### TITLE ###

A novel fluorescence “turn off-on” sensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dots in amino acid ionic liquid medium and its application


### AUTHOR GROUP ###

Wei Liu
Xiashi Zhu


### ABSTRACT ###

The spectrum behavior of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) in 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tryptophan ionic liquid (TryIL) was studied. It was showed that TryIL could self-aggregate into water/oil micellar in aqueous solution and N-GQD was assembled on hydrophilic part of TryIL micelle to construct a novel fluorescence “turn on-off” probe (N-GQD-TryIL). the fluorescence intensity was quenched through the formation of N-GQD-TryIL-Cu(II) complex due to the strong binding ability between Cu2+ and N-GQD-TryIL. In the following fluorescence “turn-on” step, the chiral ligand exchange between levodopa (LD) and Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL to partly recovery the fluorescence intensity. Corresponding influencing factors and mechanisms in fluorescence “turn-off” and “turn-on” process were discussed. Thus, a sensitive and selective fluorescence “turn off” and “turn on” platform for assay Cu(II) and LD was designed. Under the optimal conditions, Cu(II) and LD exhibited good linear relationship in the range of 0.051–16.00 μg/mL and 0.55–5.00 μg/mL. The LOD of them was 0.017 μg/mL and 0.16 μg/mL respectively. This method was successfully applied to the determination of Cu(II) and LD in real samples. Moreover, N-GQD-TryIL performed application prospects in bio-imaging due to protein affinity of the hydrophilic part.


### ABSTRACT ###



### GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT ###

Fluorescence “trun-off” and “turn-on” properties fx1


### KEYWORDS ###

N-doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Ionic liquid
Cu(II)
Levodopa


### INTRODUCTION ###

Fluorescent “turn-off-on” materials, as an important subject in fluorescence sensing, their fluorescence could be reversibly quenched and recovered under environmental stimulation [1], and they are generally regulated by light, electricity, temperature, machinery and chemistry [2]. Recent evidences indicate that MOFs, poly-acid compounds, organic dyes and quantum dots are the most popular materials for fluorescent “turn-off-on” [3–6]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are zero-dimensional (0 D) nanoparticle with a diameter less than 20 nm. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs are more environmentally friendly, biocompatible and photo-stable, which widely applied in fluorescence and electrochemical sensing and bio-imaging [4–7]. However, GQDs usually exhibit polycrystalline or highly defective structures with poor optical properties [8]. It has been paid marvelous attention to modify GQD's spectral properties. Wherein, the surface functionalization and element doping were generally employed [9]. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms, provides an attractive mean that effectively tune their intrinsic properties and improve luminous efficiency [10,11].
Recent evidences showed that external medium (such as surfactants, supramolecular, ionic liquids) posed significant influence on quantum dots’ size, composite behavior or electron/energy transfer mechanism [12–15]. Ionic liquids surfactants, which possess properties comparable to amphiphies, including micelle formation and surface activity when the carbon atoms in their hydrophobic tail reached a certain value, could dramatically change the polarity and dielectric constant of the system, which made them widely used in fluorescence sensitization research [16]. Amino acid ionic liquids (AAILs) were serials novel ionic liquids that derived from natural amino acid. Compared with conventional ionic liquids, AAILs had strong polarities, hydrogen bonding basicity, stable chiral center and better biocompatibility [17–19]. However, few researches focused on the ionic liquids (ionic liquids surfactants, amino acid ionic liquids) to improve fluorescence behavior of quantum dots.
In this paper, 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tryptophan ionic liquid (TryIL) was synthesized and utilized as medium to disperse N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) for the first time. The spectrum behavior of N-GQD in TryIL medium (N-GQD-TryIL) was studied. It could be considered as a fluorescence “turn off” and “turn on” probe for the determination of Cu(II) and Levodopa (LD). Moreover, the application of N-GQD-TryIL in protein imaging was preliminarily studied.


### EXPERIMENT ###



### REAGENTS AND INSTRUMENTS ###

Copper standard solution (1.000 μg/mL): 1.0 mL standard solution (100.0 μg/mL，China) was accurately pipetted and 5.0 mL 10% nitric acid was added into 100.0 mL Volumetric flask. LD solution (200.0 μg/mL): 0.020 g levodopa were accurately weighed, fixed volume of 50.0 mL and stored in 4 °C. Citric acid, ammonia, 1-methylimidazole, bromo-n-hexane, anhydrous ethanol was all analytical purity and provided by Sinopharm Group Regent Co. Ltd., China. TryIL was synthesized by reference method [17,20]. The buffer solution of this experiment was configured by dipotassium hydrogen phosphate and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (Sinopharm Group Regent Co. Ltd, China). The water used throughout this experiment was grade 3 water (resistance >20 MΩ).
Fluorescence spectrum was performed on F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Japan). Thremo-1780 UV Spectrophotometer (USA) was used for UV test. Shimadzu LC-10A High Performance Liquid Chromatography (Japan) was used for Pharmacopoeia method. DK-S22 Constant Temperature Water Bath (Shanghai Precision Instruments Corporation), JEM-2100 TEM (Germany) and Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Germany) were used for synthesis and characterization.


### N-GQD PREPARATION ###

N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of citric acid with ammonia according to the previous reports [21]. 4.20 g of citric acid, 40.0 mL ultrapure water, 4.50 mL of ammonia were transferred into a 100.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The resultant green mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter and dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h. Then the obtained liquid was diluted with water acquiring N-GQDs stock solution with adsorption 0.50 at 275 nm. Finally, the as-prepared N-GQDs were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and dispersion.


### PREPARATION OF N-GQD-TRYIL ###

50.0 mL N-GQDs stock solution, 0.050 g NaOH, and 0.050 g TryIL was mixed and stirred to clarification, then this mixture was heated under 40 °C in the water bath. After 1 h, the solution changed from green to bright yellow. Then this solution was freeze drying at −86 °C.


### DETERMINATION OF CU(II) BY FLUORESCENCE “TURN-OFF” METHOD ###

0.050 g N-GQD-TryIL was accurately weighed, dissolved and diluted to 100 mL. For fluorescent “turn-off” sensing of Cu(II), 2.00 mL N-GQD-TryIL, 5.00 mL pH = 6.0 buffer solutions, 2.50 mL15% NaF solutions and different volumes of Cu(II) were mixed into a 25.0 mL volumetric flask. The fluorescence spectrum of the solution under excitation at 318 nm was measured at 0 °C, and the peak intensity was recorded (both excitation and emission slits were 5.0 nm, scanning speed was 1200 nm/min).


### DETERMINATION OF LD BY FLUORESCENCE TURN ON METHOD ###

2.00 mL of N-GQD-TryIL solution, 3.00 mL Cu(II) standard solution (5.00 μg/mL), and 1.00 mL test solution was transferred into 25.00 mL volumetric flasks. After sufficient shaking, this solution was kept at 60 °C. The fluorescence spectrum was excited at 318 nm and the peak intensity was recorded (both excitation and emission slits were 5.0 nm and scan speed was 1200 nm/min).


### PREPARATION OF REAL SAMPLES ###



### ORE, SOIL SAMPLES ###

0.20 g ore/soil sample was accurately weighed and transferred into microwave digestion tank, then 1.00 mL HNO3, 3.00 mL HCl and 0.50 mL 30% hydrogen peroxide solution was added. This solution was microwave digestion at 150 °C (temperature rise time 15 min) for 30 min. Then the solution was drained of excess acid at 110 °C and the remaining solution was diluted with water to 25.0 mL.


### LEVODOPA TABLETS ###

10 pieces of levodopa(LD) tables were grinded and mixed, 0.020 g powder was weighed and placed in a 100.0 mL volumetric flask. Then 50.0 mL dilute hydrochloric acid solution was added, shook to dissolve LD.


### VALIDATION PROTOCOL ###

The calibration techniques were evaluated for Cu(II) (0.051–16.00 μg/mL) and LD(0.55–5.00 μg/mL) in aqueous solution. The linearity of the analytical curves was determined applying a linear regression analysis [22].
The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were evaluated with three and ten times baseline noise (n = 11) divided by the slope of the linear equation from the analytical curve.
The precision was evaluated with repeatability tests and reproducibility tests. The repeatability tests were estimated through of relative standard deviation (RSD), which was detected at cCu = 1.0 μg/mL−1 and cLD = 2.0 μg/mL−1 for 5 times. The reproducibility tests were performed by RSD determined at cCu = 1.0 μg/mL−1 and cLD = 2.0 μg/mL−1 for 5 days [22].
The interference experiment was to analysis for Cu(II) in presence of different concentration of Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Na+ and K+, and LD coexisting with dopamine, aspartic acid, glutathione and phenylalanine. When the relative error caused by the interferer reached ± 5.0% [20], the ratio (cinterferer/canalyte) was recorded as interference factor (IF). The larger IF meant better anti-interference ability of this method.
Accuracy of the method was checked using recovery tests with different concentration of the Cu(II) and LD standard solution added into sample solution. And t-test at 95% confidence level was applied to verify the statistical significance of the result obtained by this method and standard certified value or values by standard method.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###



### DISPERSION BEHAVIOR OF N-GQD IN TRYIL ###

The critical micelle concentration (CMC), zeta potentials and TEM were discussed in the current experiment to characterize the formation of TryIL micelles and the dispersion behavior of N-GQD in TryIL medium.


### CMC, ZETA POTENTIALS ###

CMC is the lowest concentration at which ionic liquids associate in water to form micelles [23]. In this work, CMC values of TryIL and N-GQD-TryIL were determined by conductivity method. As is shown in Fig. 1, the CMC of TryIL is 0.32%, namely TryIL was self-aggregated into micellar when the concentration of TryIL is greater than 0.32%. The CMC of TryIL was decreased to 0.28% when N-GQD was added. It could be speculated that there might be an interaction between TryIL and N-GQD
The zeta potentials of TryIL and N-GQD-TryIL was determined in this experiment. the zeta potential of TryIL moved toward positive from −45.3 mV to −28.7 mV by the addition of N-GQD. The reason may be that N-GQD interacted with TryIL due to the surface charge of TryIL was partly neutralized by N-GQD and N-GQD would be attached onto the surface of TryIL through electrostatic interaction according to the results revealed above [14].


### TEM CHARACTERIZATION ###

TEM and HR-TEM were used to character the morphology of N-GQD and N-GQD-TryIL ( Fig. 2). Fig. 2A, B were TEM, HR-TEM images of N-GQD and C, D of N-GQD-TryIL. Following conclusions could be drawn: 1) N-GQD has been successfully synthesized by their shapes and crystal lattice spacing (0.215 nm); 2) N-GQD were successfully attached to the surface of TryIL micelles.


### THEORETICAL CALCULATION OF N-GQD-TRYIL ###

According to the energy minimum principle, the interaction between N-GQD and TryIL could be simulated by theoretical calculation [20]. The simulation N-GQD and TryIL was displayed in Fig. S1A and B. They were merged and calculated under PM 6 level (scrf = PCM, solvent = water). The resultant morphology was shown in Fig. S1C. It could be inferred that N-GQD could interact with TryIL though π-π interactions and hydrogen bond interactions. This ordered combination of molecules might give N-GQD-TryIL different spectral properties.


### SPECTRAL PROPERTIES OF N-GQD-TRYIL ###

In this section, three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectra, ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime of N-GQD and N-GQD-TryIL were compared to invest the newly spectral behavior.


### FLUORESCENCE SPECTRA ###

Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of N-GQD (A) and N-GQD-TryIL (B) were displayed in Fig. 3. It could be learnt that the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of N-GQD-TryIL were significantly red-shift comparing with N-GQD. This phenomenon might be owing to the reduction of N-GQD aggregation in the micellar environment, causing the red-shift emission wavelength [15].
Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQD（C）and N-GQD-TryIL(D) at different excitation wavelengths were presented in Fig. 3. The results stated that: (1) N-GQD was excitation-dependent, while N-GQD-TryIL was excitation-independent. N-GQD-TryIL was suitable acted as a fluorescence probe at a certain wavelength [24]; (2) The optimum excitation wavelength for N-GQD-TryIL was 318 nm, and the emission wavelength was 421 nm; (3) The fluorescence intensity could be affected by N-GQD-TryIL concentration. (Fig. 3E)

### UV ABSORPTION SPECTRUM ###

The UV absorption spectrum of N-GQD (H2O as a reference, curve 1) and N-GQD-TryIL (TryIL at the same concentration as a reference, curve 2) were shown in Fig. S2. The maximum absorption of N-GQD peak appeared at 276 nm and 315 nm owing to π-π* and n-π* transition. The maximum absorption of N-GQD-TryIL was significantly differed and red shifted to 282 nm and 337 nm. It might be owing to electrostatic interactions between N-GQD and TryIL [25]. And the results was consistent with 3.1.3.


### FLUORESCENCE QUANTUM YIELDS (ΦF), TIME STABILITY ###

The Φf of N-GQD and N-GQD-TryIL were detected according to reference method [18] (Quinine sulfate was used as a reference with a standard Φf = 55%). The Φf of N-GQD-TryIL (37%) was significant higher than N-GQD (26%). This phenomenon might be owing to the reduction of polycrystalline structure in TryIL medium. The time stability of N-GQD-TryIL was represented by fluorescence intensity within seven days, and the fluorescence intensity were kept steady (RSD = 0.17%), which illustrated that N-GQD-TryIL was stable enough as a fluorescence probe.


### FLUORESCENCE LIFETIME ###

The fluorescence decay curves of were N-GQD (curve 1) and N-GQD-TryIL (curve 2) performed in Fig. S3. Lifetimes (τ1) were calculated by fitting the curves with multiexponential equations described as follows [15]: I ( t ) = A 1 e − t / τ 1 + A 2 e − t / τ 2 τ = A 1 τ 1 2 + A 2 τ 2 2 A 1 τ 1 + A 2 τ 2
As a consequence, the fluorescence decay curves of N-GQD and N-GQD-TryIL were single exponential decay and similar fluorescence lifetime (N-GQD: 3.12 ns, N-GQD-TryIL: 3.40 ns)
In summary, the spectral behavior of N-GQD-TryIL was significantly differed. The N-GQD in TryIL medium had non-excitation wavelength-dependent, higher luminous efficiency, and stability. These advantages made N-GQD-TryIL suitable as a probe for fluorescence sensing and protein imaging.


### N-GQD-TRYIL FLUORESCENCE “TURN OFF/ON PROPERTIES” ###

In this experiment, different concentrations of Cu(II) standard solution were added to the N-GQD-TryIL solution, and the N-GQD-TryIL fluorescence spectrum was recorded under excited wavelength 318 nm. The results were shown in Fig. 4A. The fluorescence intensity continuously “turned off” with the addition of Cu(II). As the LD was added into quenched solution, the fluorescence spectrum of N-GQD-TryIL was partly turned on under the same wavelength (Fig. 4B). Moreover the value of fluorescence “turn-on” accounted for 1/3 of total “turn-off” value. Therefore, N-GQD-TryIL could be employed as a fluorescent “turn off” and “turn on” probe for the quantitative detection of Cu(II) and LD. (Fig. 4C).


### INFLUENCING FACTORS OF FLUORESCENCE “TURN-OFF” PROCESSES ###

The pH, temperature, and ionic strength was the main factors affecting the fluorescence “turn-off” process. The pH (4.0–9.0), temperature (0–60 °C) and ionic strength (0–2.5%) were invested in current experiment respectively. Corresponding fluorescence “turn-off” intensity (ΔF1) was recorded in Fig. S4. The following conclusions could be drawn: 1) As the pH increased, ΔF1 increased firstly, then decreased, and tended to be stable between pH 6.0 and 8.0 (Fig. S4 A). This might result from that the surface functional group of N-GQD-TryIL was deprotonated and more likely to interact with Cu(II) at pH ranging from 6.0 to 8.0. When the pH was larger than 8.0, the hydroxyl side reaction coefficient Cu(II) increased, making it difficult interact with N-GQD-TryIL and leading a decreasing of ΔF1. 2) As the system temperature increased, ΔF1 decreased continuously (Fig. S4 B). It might be explained that the stability of Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL was declined along with the temperature arising. 3) When the solution ionic strength (NaF concentration) was greater than 2.0%, ΔF1 decreased, which was due to the enhancement in NaF concentration causing colloidal coagulation. (Fig. S4 C)

### INFLUENCING FACTORS OF FLUORESCENCE TURN ON PROCESS ###

In order to investigate the influence factors of fluorescence turn on process. The intensity of fluorescence “turn-on” (ΔF2) in the range of 5.0–25.0 μg/mL Cu(II) and 0–60 °C were studied. The result was shown in Fig. S5. It could be drawn that the ΔF2 was gradually decreased as the Cu(II) content increasing (Fig. S5A). This phenomenon might be owing to the increasing of Cu(II) concentration, which leads to LD preferentially binding to Cu(II) instead of ligand-exchanging with Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL. When the experimental temperature increased, ΔF2 gradually increased (Fig. S5 B). It could be explained by the decreasing in the stability of Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL.


### EFFECT OF INTERFERING SUBSTANCES ON FLUORESCENCE “TURN-OFF” AND “TURN-ON” ###

The IF of multiple metal ion were performed in Table S1. As the concentration of Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cr3+ and Mg2+ reached 5, 8, 12, 20 and 35 times the concentration of Cu(II), they posed influence on Cu(II) detection with 5% relative error [26]. Therefor this method was still practical in most real samples. The same method was employed to invest the interference on fluorescence “turn-on” process. However, there were scarcely substance had influence on the “turn on” process.


### MECHANISM EXPLANATIONS OF FLUORESCENCE “TURN-OFF” AND “TURN-ON” PROCESS ###

The mechanism of N-GQD-TryIL fluorescence “turn off” and “turn on” was studied by thermodynamic parameters, infrared spectroscopy, particle size distribution and theoretical calculations.


### THERMODYNAMIC PARAMETER CALCULATION ###

The quenching/recovery constant Ksv was brought into the Van't Hoff equation at different temperatures, and the ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG during fluorescence turn off/on process were calculated by fitting formulas as follow: F 0 △ F = 1 f a K a [ Q ] + 1 f 1 In K = − ( △ H 0 RT ) + ( △ S 0 R ) △ G 0 = △ H 0 − T △ S 0
The results were shown in Table S2 The following conclusions could be drawn: 1) ΔH ≈ 0, ΔS > 0 indicated that electrostatic interaction might exist during the rurn off/on process [27]; 2) ΔG < 0 indicated that the fluorescence turn off/on was spontaneous.


### INFRARED SPECTRUM ###

The shifting of absorption in the infrared spectrum could reflect the interaction between two species [28]. The infrared spectrum of N-GQD-TryIL and LD with the present or absent of Cu(II) was shown in Fig. S6 A and B. In the presence of Cu2+, the stretching peak of the N-H bond red-shifted, and the C=O stretching peak blue-shifted. It indicated that Cu2+ interacted with the N, O atoms on the surface amino acid of N-GQD-TryIL and LD.


### PARTICLE SIZE DISTRIBUTION ###

Particle size distribution of N-GQD-TryIL, Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL, Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL+LD could be used to determine the microstructure. The particle size analysis results were displayed in Fig. S7. The N-GQD-TryIL was 139.8 nm, but significantly increased to 588.1 nm resulting from the formation of Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL complex through aggregation kinetics mechanism. In the presence of LD, the particle size of the system was reduced (539.4 nm), and a new particle (152 nm), which was close to the size of the N-GQD-TryIL, appeared. Thus the mechanism of turn on could be explained as a ligand exchange process between LD and Cu (II)-N-GQD-TryIL complex to dissociate the complex and partly “turn-on” fluorescence. According to the literature reports [29,30], it could be inferred that Cu(II) might form distorted octahedral complex Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL with three N-GQD-TryIL. The LD molecule underwent a ligand exchange process with Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL to displace an N-GQD-TryIL to turn on the fluorescent of the system partly.


### THEORETICAL CALCULATION ###

In order to verify the formation of Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL and ligand exchange process, DFT theory was used to molecularly simulate the possible morphology at the PM6 level through Gaussian 09 [20]. Under the lowest energy conformation ( Fig. 5), the empty orbit of Cu(II) could interact with N and O in N-GQD-TryIL. This phenomenon was consistent with the increasing size of the Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL in fluorescence turn-off. With the presence of LD，Cu(II)-N-GQD-TryIL was partly dissociated through ligand exchange and the fluorescence of the system was turned on. This conclusion was basically consistent with experiment results in 3.3 and 3.7.3. As a consequence, the chemical reaction between N-GQD-TryIL, Cu(II) and LD might be described as Fig. 6.


### ANALYSIS APPLICATION ###

According to the result in Sections 3.3 and 3.4, the optimal conditions in the determination of Cu(II) and LD was summarized in Table S3. The analysis application was performed under those conditions.


### ANALYSIS PERFORMANCE ###

The analysis performance for quantitative analysis of Cu(II) and LD in this method was displayed in Table 1. The method exhibited satisfactory sensitivity and stability. This analytical performances were compared with reported methods ( Table 2). It indicated that this method had the advantages of better stability compared with the developed method and the sensitivity (LOD) was in the range of the value in reported methods.


### REAL SAMPLES ###

The results of Cu(II) and LD in real samples were shown in Tables 3, 4. The recovery rates of Cu(II) in water samples, ore and soil samples were ranged in 90.8–102.1%, 95.7–104.4%, and 88.5–115.4% respectively. The Cu(II) assay result in the standard water sample (BWZ6616-2016, standard value 0.999 μg/mL) was 0.987 ± 0.02 μg/mL, and there was no significant difference between this result and the standard value. The ore standard sample (GBW07164, standard value 2.80%) was 2.89 ± 0.09% by this method and there was no significant difference between this result and the standard value (t-test).
The recovery rates of LD in the drug and urine samples was 93.7–102.2%. Wherein the drug sample had a consequence of 13.9 mg/g, which was consisted with the results of the pharmacopoeia method (14.1 mg/g, t-test). The results showed that the method could be employed to determine the contents of Cu(II) and LD in various samples with satisfactory analytical results.


### PROTEIN IMAGING ###

The hydrophilic part of N-GQD-TryIL was constructed by amino acid, N-GQD-TryIL has a good application prospect in the field of protein imaging. In this experiment, bovine serum albumin was invoked as a template protein and the imaging results was shown in Fig. 7. Wherein, 1a-1d was bovine serum albumin without N-GQD-TryIL. 2a-2d was pictures of bovine serum albumin after addition of N-GQD only. The bovine serum albumin was difficult to observe under different wavelengths of illumination. 3a-3d and 4a-4d was the bovine serum albumin after adding N-GQD-TryIL. The template protein could be clearly observed under different objective magnification and N-GQD-TryIL could meet the requirements of simultaneous multicolor fluorescent protein imaging. Hence, N-GQD-TryIL had potential application in the field of protein imaging comparing with N-GQD owing to its protein affinity.


### CONCLUSION ###

In summary, N-GQD were firstly dispersed in TryIL medium, and the spectral properties was changed greatly. N-GQD-TryIL could be used as a fluorescence “turn off-on” probe modulated by Cu(II) and LD. Wherein Cu(II) was quencher and LD was fluorescence recovery agent. The method was successfully applied to quantitative analysis of Cu(II) and LD in multiple samples and achieved satisfactory results. Moreover, N-GQD-TryIL had useful application prospects in the field of protein imaging.

Article_Content:
### TITLE ###

Target-triggered in situ autocatalysis in nanopore membrane for point-of-care testing of sub-nanomolar Ag+


### FIG. 1: (A) Construction of a 3D POCT device consisting of two layers of function thin membranes (patterned with hollow structures via laser cutting) with wax-loaded non-functional zones: (1) polycarbonate nanopore membrane (PNM) having a circular function zone a deposited with 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB); and (2) nitrocellulose membrane having a circular function zone b immobilized with red ink and a rectangular function zone c. The red arrow indicates the laser-cut hollow bar mark of the zone c’s 1 cm-length. The device is packaged between two pieces of transparent adhesive tape. The red dotted box indicates a small piece of transparent adhesive tape that covers the top of zone a. Scale bar is 5 mm. (B) Schematic diagram illustrating the working principle of the POCT device for timing detection of Ag+ ions that integrates target-triggered in situ autocatalysis for signal amplification with a phone for instrument-free, quantitative readout (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). ###


### FIG. 2: (A) UV–vis spectra of two 5 mM TMB solutions (25 μL each) mixed with 25 μL of (1) a blank sample (without analyte) and (2) a 13 nM Ag+ sample. The inset shows photographs of the two mixtures above. (B) Corresponding TEM image and EDS result of the No. 2 blue mixture solution shown in (A). The scale bar is 200 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). ###


### FIG. 3: Representative images obtained from analysis of (A) a blank sample (i.e., water without analyte) and (C) a 13 nM Ag+ sample at different time of red ink’s flowing. Corresponding SEM images of zones a of the PNMs used in the assays (A) and (C) were given in (B) and (D), respectively. Each scale bar is 100 nm. The red arrows indicate typical examples of silver nanoparticles formed. The typical EDS result was provided as the inset in (D) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). ###


### FIG. 4: (A) Results of flowing time (T) of red ink obtained from the blank sample and different metal ion samples: Ag+, 13 nM; other ions, 10 μM. (B) Relationship between the flowing time changes (ΔT) of red ink obtained from different Ag+ samples and the analyte concentrations (C Ag+). The inset displays the calibration curve with a regression equation: ΔT (s) = 2.8609C Ag+ + 13.1333 (R = 0.9984). Each data point is an average of three replicate experiments and error bars indicate standard deviations (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). ###


### TITLE ###

Target-triggered in situ autocatalysis in nanopore membrane for point-of-care testing of sub-nanomolar Ag+


### AUTHOR GROUP ###

Haiyan Fu
Qiaobo Yin
Huili Wang
Jinfang Nie
Wencheng Xiao
Zhaoying Liu
Yijing Chen
Yun Zhang


### ABSTRACT ###

This work initially describes a sample-in-answer-out paper-based assay for simple, rapid, sensitive, specific, and low-cost detection of silver (I) (Ag+) ions. It combines Ag+-triggered in situ autocatalysis for signal amplification with an instrument-free, timing readout in 3D origami paper-based analytical devices integrated with nanopore membranes (NMs). The autocatalytic amplification is based on the oxidability of Ag+ towards 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) loaded in the NMs. The Ag+ ions are rapidly reduced by the TMB to form silver nanoparticles that further act as nanocatalysts to catalyse the oxidation of more TMB molecules to their derivatives by Ag+ ions for producing bigger (and more) metal particles. The NM’s pores are thus partially blocked by these metal particles to adjust (prolong) the flowing time of a coloured reagent in the paper device. Under optimized conditions, this developed point-of-need method could quantitatively detect the Ag+ ions in a linear concentration range of 1.9–62.5 nM within 4 min, by simply using a ubiquitous phone (or a timer). Its detection limit was estimated to be ˜0.91 nM according to the 3σ rule. The recoveries of analyzing real water examples (i.e., pond water, tap water, drinking water, and soil water) were from ˜95.2 to ˜103.9%, and the corresponding relative standard deviations ranged from 3.3 to 8.1% (n = 6).


### KEYWORDS ###

Heavy metal ions
Point-of-care testing
Signal amplification
Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices
Instrument-free detection
Silver ion


### INTRODUCTION ###

With rapid, increasing global industrializations, the contamination of water by heavy metal ions has become one of the most serious environmental problems facing the world today [1]. Silver (I) (Ag+) ion belongs to the highest toxicity class of heavy metal pollutants, together with cadmium, chromium (VI), copper, and mercury [2]. As it is widely used in the electronic, pharmaceutical, and photographic industries, the annual amount of Ag+ released to the surface water from industrial wastes and emissions has been estimated at ca. 80 tons 40 years ago [2]. According to the reports of US Environmental Protection Agency, the Ag+ ion at levels above 1.6 nM is toxic to fishes and microorganisms, and its maximum acceptable concentration in drinking water is restricted to 0.9 μM [3]. The excessive Ag+ intake would cause argyria in humans and damage nervous, renal, immune, and cardiovascular systems.4−6 Therefore, these environmental and health problems caused by the Ag+ have prompted researchers to develop efficient methods for sensitive, specific detection of this contaminant for environmental protection, waste management, and water safety.
The existing toolbox toward this goal for the Ag+ detection mainly includes traditional techniques such as atomic absorption and emission spectrometry [7,8] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9] and some recently established methods, e.g., fluorescence spectrometry (with organic dyes [10] or inorganic quantum dots such as carbon dots [11]), absorption spectrometry (typically adopting gold nanoparticles as colorimetric probes [12,13]), electrochemical sensors [14,15], and paper-based test strips [16,17]. The instrumental analysis techniques based on spectrometry and electrochemistry can offer high sensitivity, but most of them may suffer from the drawbacks of bulky, complicated, costly instrumentation, or time-consuming sample pretreatment processes. Moreover, the paper strips for Ag+ disclosed by far just enabled qualitative analysis of nanomolar analyte under tedious and precise control of reaction temperatures. Thus, it still remains a challenge to develop more desirable assays that allow for simple, rapid, economic, and portable detection and quantification of Ag+ at nanomolar levels to meet the growing demand of on-site or point-of-need water monitoring by limitedly-trained personnel or even unskilled home-users.
In this communication, we respond to this challenge by designing a type of 3D point-of-care testing (POCT) device for instrument-free timing assay of sub-nanomolar Ag+ ions that uses a target-triggered in situ autocatalysis strategy for facile, efficient signal amplification and a ubiquitous phone for portable quantitative readout. Detailed experimental section was provided in Supplementary Material. The construction of such a POCT device is shown in Fig. 1 A. Each device is composed of two layers of function thin membranes that are patterned with hollow structures via laser cutting [18]. The layer 1 is a piece of polycarbonate nanopore membrane (PNM, ˜50 nm in average pore size) that has a 5 mm-diameter circular function zone a. The layer 2 is a piece of nitrocellulose membrane that has a 5 mm-diameter circular function zone b and a 1 mm × 2.5 cm rectangular function zone c. The 1 cm-length part of zone c (beginning from its circular part) is marked with a 1 mm-length hollow bar cut by laser (as indicated by a red arrow in Fig. 1A). Supporting (non-functional) zones of the two hollow structure-patterned membranes are hydrophobized with wax [19]. The zones a and b are loaded with 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and a coloured reagent (red ink in this case), respectively. The two layers of reagent-loaded function membranes are assembled into a 3D device and are further sandwiched (packaged) by two pieces of transparent adhesive tape. Note that, the top of zone a in the layer 1 is covered solely with a small piece of adhesive tape that can be taken off for sample introduction (as indicated by a red dotted box in Fig. 1A).
As illustrated in Fig. 1B, our POCT device for Ag+ assay involves two key operations: 1) addition of a sample solution to trigger the in situ autocatalysis for producing silver nanoparticles in the PNM; and 2) measurement of the time required for the red ink (re-dissolved in zone b by reaction solution) flowing to the hollow bar mark in the POCT device using a phone. The “autocatalysis” refers to that the Ag+ ions are reduced by the TMB to form silver nanoparticles that in turn automatically catalyze the oxidation of more TMB molecules to their derivatives (TMBox) by Ag+ ions. As a result, bigger (and more) metal particles can be formed. The as-formed metal particles could partially block the PNM’s nanopores, thus prolonging the flowing time of red ink. The number of blocked pores in the PNM and the ink’s flowing time positively rely on the Ag+ concentration in the sample. The results demonstrate that the proposed instrument-free method only needs a ubiquitous phone (or a commercially available, far cheaper timer) to sensitively, selectively quantify the Ag+ levels in artificial water samples and real samples of pond water, tap water, drinking water, and soil water within several minutes. In particular, amplified detection of Ag+ with a limit less than 1 nM could be achieved, which makes our assay one of the most sensitive Ag+ sensing approaches compared with many other recently reported methods [4–6,10–15,20–22] (Table S1 in Supplementary Material).


### MATERIAL AND METHODS ###



### MATERIALS AND APPARATUS ###

3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and dimethylsulfoxide were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and other metal salts or acids were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Red ink is the product of Shanghai Hero Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The original red ink solution is composed of solvents, red dyes, resins, solubilizers, surfactants, and other materials. Besides the red ink, other types of coloured reagents such as blue ink and food green dye are also suitable. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Unless otherwise specified, all solutions were prepared with deionized water (with a specific resistivity >18.2 MΩ•cm) from an ultrapure water system (UPS-II-20 L) bought from Chengdu Yuechun Technology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The stock solutions of TMB (40 mM) were prepared in dimethylsulfoxide and diluted with water to required concentrations before use. The real water samples include pond water collected from a pond in our campus, tap water obtained from our lab, commercially available drinking water (the product of Hangzhou Wahaha Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China), and soil water collected from the vicinity of a factory.
Hydrophilic polycarbonate nanopore membranes (PNMs) (Whatman, ˜50 nm in average pore size, ˜3 × 108 pore/cm2 in average pore density) were the products of GE Healthcare Life Sciences. Nitrocellulose membrane (˜0.2 μm in average pore size) was from Shanghai Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mini-type CO2 laser cutting machine (XB-3020) was provided by Shangdong Xinbang Laser Equipment Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Optical characterization of reaction solutions was performed on a UV–vis spectrometer (Cary 50, Varian, USA). The morphology and element composition of nanoparticles formed in the reaction solutions were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) equipped with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The morphologies of the PNMs used in the assays of a blank sample and an Ag+ sample were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU 5000, Hitachi, Japan).


### PATTERNING OF FUNCTION THIN MEMBRANES AND IMMOBILIZATION OF TMB AND RED INK ###

For fabrication of a 3D point-of-care testing (POCT) device, a piece of PNM (2 cm × 2 cm) and a piece of nitrocellulose membrane (2 cm × 4 cm) were patterned with hollow microstructures using a previous laser cutting method [18]. The patterns were designed on a computer with Coreldraw X5 drawing software. The PNM has a 5 mm-diameter circular function zone a. The nitrocellulose membrane has a 5 mm-diameter circular function zone b and a 1 mm × 2.5 cm rectangular function zone c. The 1 cm-length of zone c beginning from the end of zone b was marked with a 1 mm-length hollow line. The one-step patterning of laser cutting was carried out in the laser machine at an applied current of 5 mA, a cutting rate of 20 mm/s, a laser wavelength of 10.64 μm, and a laser power of 40 W. After paper cutting, supporting (non-functional) zones of the two hollow structure-patterned membranes were further hydrophobized by wax [19].
Then, ˜2.5 μL of a 200 μM TMB solution and ˜2.5 μL of a red ink solution were spotted on the zone a in the PNM and the zone b in the nitrocellulose membrane, respectively, and allowed to air dry. The TMB-loaded PNM (as layer 1) and ink-loaded nitrocellulose membrane (as layer 2) were assembled into a 3D device that was further sandwiched by two pieces of transparent adhesive tape. It should be noted that the top zone a in the device was covered solely with another small piece of adhesive tape. The freshly as-prepared TMB- and ink-immobilized 3D POCT devices were stored in a sealed bag and kept away from light under room temperature. They could remain stable for at least one month without significant loss of assay sensitivity. More satisfactory storage stability might be expected if using vacuum packaging technique to package these devices.


### ASSAY PROCEDURES ###

In a typical assay, after taking off the small piece of adhesive tape that covered the top zone a of a 3D POCT device, 10 μL of an Ag+ sample solution (artificial sample in deionized water or real water sample) was added onto this function zone. Then a phone (i.e., iPhone 5 s) or a timer was immediately used to record the time required for the red ink (re-dissolved in the zone b by the reaction solution) to flow to the marker line in the zone c of the device. The response signal of flowing time change (ΔT) was defined as ΔT = Ts − Tb , where Ts and Tb were the flowing time recorded for the Ag+ sample and a blank sample (i.e., deionized water without the analyte), respectively, and used to estimate the Ag+ concentration. In addition, comparative (specificity) experiments were carried out in the same way but using other eighteen types of metal ions (i.e., Pt4+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Au3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Na+, and K+) instead of the analyte Ag+.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###

The core concept of this new assay focuses on the conversion of the Ag+ detection into the measurement of the blocked degree of PNM’s pores in the POCT device. Therefore, the analyte-triggered autocatalytic production of silver nanoparticles via the Ag+-TMB redox reactions was first verified. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the original TMB solution (5 mM) is colourless (image 1), and no meaningful absorption was measured during the tested wavelength range of 350–800 nm in its UV–vis spectrum (curve 1). As expected, the mixing of 13 nM Ag+ resulted in a blue reaction mixture (image 2); it exhibits three strong characteristic absorption peaks at 370, 458, and 659 nm (curve 2), indicating the oxidation of TMB to TMBox by Ag+ [23–25]. As additionally shown in the real-time Video S1 in Supplementary Material, the redox reactions between the Ag+ and TMB was quite fast. The accompanying colour change from colourless to blue in the solution could be clearly observed in a few seconds as soon as the mixing operation was done.
In order to understand such a fast chromogenic reaction, its resulting mixture was further characterized using both transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) techniques. As displayed in Fig. 2B, a large number of spherical or ellipsoidal silver nanoparticles had been unexpectedly generated during the Ag+-TMB redox reactions. According to this direct experimental evidence, the reason for the rapid colour change occurred in the mixture solution could be presumably explained as follows. When the Ag+ sample was mixed with the TMB solution, the analyte ions were reduced by TMB to zerovalent silvers which in turn formed small silver nanoparticles [26]. These small metal particles further served as in situ, autocatalytic, active nanoseeds capable of catalyzing more TMB molecules to their derivatives (TMBox) by Ag+ ions. Meanwhile, more Ag+ ions were reduced to zerovalent metal species, thus either making these nanocatalysts themselves grow or producing more particles [27].
After demonstrating the Ag+-triggered in situ autocatalytic formations of silver nanoparticles, the feasibility of the designed POCT devices for timing detection of Ag+ was then validated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when a blank water sample (without analyte) was analyzed, it took ˜19.3 s for the red ink solution to flow to the hollow bar mark in layer 2 of the device used (Fig. 3A, right). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the zone a in corresponding layer 1 revealed obviously that almost all of PNM’s circular nanopores kept their original “open” state facilitating the ink’s rapid passing (Fig. 3B). On the other hand, for a 13 nM Ag+ sample, no flow of red ink was observed during the 19.3-s period (Fig. 3C, middle). After ˜80.4 s, this coloured reagent finally reached the hollow bar mark (Fig. 3C, right). The far longer flowing time of red ink should be based on a fact that the Ag+-triggered autocatalysis enabled the in situ creation of lots of silver nanoparticles in the zone a of used PNM, which was clearly confirmed by its SEM and EDS results (Fig. 3D). Since these particles blocked (“closed”) many pores of PNM, the volume and flowing rate of re-dissolved red ink solution were highly limited (reduced). In addition, it was experimentally found that too high Ag+ levels (e.g., >10 μM) would lead to decreases in the ink’s flowing time in the device, while its PNM surface colour changed from white to blue because of the relatively large amount of blue TMBox produced. This unusual phenomenon may be attributed to the generation of too big silver particles in the presence of excess Ag+ ions, resulting in non-uniform blocking of nanopores in the whole body of zone a in the PNM used.
Next, to test the selectivity (specificity) of the proposed method, fifteen samples of other potential interfering metal ions in water (i.e., Pt4+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Au3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Na+, and K+; 10 μM each) were separately added into fifteen POCT devices, followed by timing measurement, comparing with the results of assaying a blank sample and a 13 nM Ag+ sample. As shown in Fig. 4 A, for Pt4+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Na+, or K+, a similar short flowing time of ca. 19.7 s was measured for each type of ion. No meaningful difference was observed between such time response and that obtained from the blank sample (ca. 19.3 s). Moreover, a relatively long flowing time of red ink (ca. 21.1 s) was recorded for other eleven types of interfering ions. In the Ag+ assay, however, a long flowing time up to ˜80.7 s was obtained, although the analyte level (13 nM) was about three orders of magnitude lower than that of the tested non-specific ions (10 μM). The data implied that only the Ag+ analyte could specifically trigger the in situ autocatalytic formations of silver nanoparticles to efficiently block the PNM’s nanopores in the used POCT device. Moreover, it was interesting to find that the TMB’s oxidation by Fe3+ and Au3+ ions producing TMBox could also result in two blue mixture solutions (insets of Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material). Nevertheless, the red ink’s flowing time as low as ˜21.2 s recorded for the two types of ions along with the results of scanning electron microscope image (Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material) suggested that there were no (or negligible) metal nanoparticles generated in the zones a of involved PNMs.
Before the evaluation of the analytical ability of our devices, the main experimental factors have been studied and optimized, including TMB volume and concentration and sample volume. The flowing time change (ΔT) of red ink between an Ag+ sample tested (7 nM) and a blank sample was calculated for each experiment. Figs. S2 and S3 in Supplementary Material show the effects of the volume and concentration of TMB loaded in PNMs on the proposed Ag+ assay. One can find that there were no obvious increases in ΔT values when the TMB volume and concentration were more than 1.5 μL and 5 mM, respectively, which were recommended for the PNM modification. Moreover, as can be seen in Fig. S4 in Supplementary Material, the sample volume could also play an important role in the analysis. When the volume of Ag+ sample increased from 6 to 10 μL, the final amount of Ag particles generated in the PNMs greatly increased allowing ΔT values to arise from 5.5 up to 33.1. However, the continuing increase in sample volumes, i.e., from 10 to 14 μL, enabled the formation of too large particles with ΔT values changing from 33.1 down to 27.2. The optimal sample volume of 10 μL was thereby chosen for all of the experiments.
Then, a series of artificial water samples with varying Ag+ concentrations were assayed under optimal conditions. The ink’s ΔT value between each Ag+ sample and a blank sample was calculated. As shown in Fig. 4B, as the analyte level increases from 1.9 to 96 nM, the ΔT increases from 15.1 to 196.2 s, suggesting Ag+-controlled timing responses of the coloured reagent. The inset of Fig. 4B additionally presents that our assay is linearly sensitive to the Ag+ target in the artificial samples in a concentration range of 1.9–62.5 nM. The detection limit for Ag+ was estimated to be ca. 0.91 nM (3σ). In comparison with some recently reported quantitative Ag+ assays with the fluorescence [4–6,10], absorbance [12,13,20,21], or electrochemistry [14,15,22] measurements, our new method is simpler and cheaper, allows limitedly-trained personnel (or even unskilled home-users) to achieve comparable or even better sensitivity, and especially offers a POCT capability for on-site uses (Table S1 in Supplementary Material).
The accuracy and applicability of the analytical device described herein was also assessed by conducting recovery tests using several real water samples, namely pond water collected from a pond on campus, tap water obtained from our lab, commercially available drinking water, and soil water collected from the vicinity of a factory. Before the analysis, all the water samples were filtrated with PNMs to remove potential interferences. All the tests were performed six times and the results were summarized in Table S2 (Supplementary Material). One can find from Table S2 that recovery results obtained are in a range of 95.2–103.9% and relative standard deviations range from 3.3 to 8.1% (n = 6). The high recovery percentages and small standard deviations in the experiments validate high accuracy and practicability of our devices for specific identification and quantification of Ag+ in real complex matrices such as environmental water samples.
Moreover, ambient temperature and sunlight are two important factors which have to be considered when performing in-field Ag+ detection. As displayed in Fig. S5 in Supplementary Material, quite similar red ink’s flowing time was obtained at tested ambient temperatures ranging from 4 to 50 ℃. Negligible differences were also observed in the results of assaying the same sets of Ag+ samples in sunlight and indoors (Fig. S6 in Supplementary Material). The ignorable influences of ambient temperature and sunlight on the performance of this new assay may be due to capillary action-driven rapid reactions in the hydrophilic paper body. It should be noted that each of reagent-loaded paper devices freshly prepared should be stored in a single seal bag and kept away from light. They could remain stable for at least one month without significant loss of analytical sensitivity. More satisfactory storage stability might be expected if using vacuum packaging technique to package these devices.


### CONCLUSIONS ###

We have developed a new type of 3D POCT device with the phone readout for instrument-free timing detection and quantification of sub-nanomolar Ag+ ions within several minutes. We attested that such rapid, enhanced Ag+-dependant timing responses may result from highly efficient target-triggered in situ autocatalysis amplification effect. That is, this effect allowed for the in situ production of lots of silver nanoparticles, which could, in turn, catalyze the oxidation of more TMB molecules to their derivatives by Ag+ ions. As a result, bigger (and more) metal particles can be formed to block nanopores of the PNM integrated into the device. Because of its simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, and sample-in-answer-out capability, our POCT devices should find wide field-based applications for water monitoring, particularly in various resource-poor environments.

### TITLE ###

Ratiometric fluorescence based on silver clusters and N, Fe doped carbon dots for determination of H2O2 and UA: N, Fe doped carbon dots as mimetic peroxidase


### AUTHOR GROUP ###

Guizhou Yue
Suying Li
Wei Liu
Fang Ding
Ping Zou
Xianxiang Wang
Qingbiao Zhao
Hanbing Rao
The first two authors contributed equally to this work and are joint first authors.


### ABSTRACT ###



### GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT ###

In this sensing system, uricase catalyzes UA to produce H2O2, and the fluorescent carbon dots with peroxidase activity were used to catalyze H2O2, generating hydroxyl radicals to etch silver clusters.


### ABSTRACT ###

In this study, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensing system is developed for the analysis of H2O2 and uric acid (UA). This sensing system is based on N, Fe-doped carbon dots (N, Fe-CDs) and bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized silver nanoclusters (BSA-Ag NCs) with peroxidase activity. By the catalysis of uriase, UA was oxidized by oxygen dissolved in the solution and H2O2 is produced. The carbon site with peroxidase activity can catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals, which etches to the BSA-Ag NCs. At the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, there were dual emission peaks at 450 nm and 650 nm. With increasing concentration of H2O2 or UA, the red fluorescence at 650 nm for BSA-Ag NCs decreases due to the etching of BSA-Ag NCs by hydroxyl radicals, while the blue fluorescence of N, Fe-CDs is restored. Based on the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450/I650) to detect H2O2 and UA, the linear range of H2O2 was 0.6–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.30 μM (S/N = 3), while the linear range of UA was 0.8–133 μM with a detection limit of 0.49 μM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the present method was used for the analysis of UA in human urine with satisfactory accuracy as well as recovery. This ratiometric fluorescent approach can be used for analysis of UA and H2O2 for biomedical applications.


### KEYWORDS ###

Ag nanoclusters
Inner filter effect
Hydroxyl radicals
Peroxidase
Uric acid


### INTRODUCTION ###

As the main product of purine metabolism in human [1], uric acid (UA) is slightly soluble in water and the normal amount of UA in human urine is around 1.49–4.46 mM [2]. Abnormal UA content will do harm to human’s health and lead to various diseases, such as gout, kidney stones, hypertension, hyperuricemia, cardiovascular disease, and Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, etc [3]. Therefore, UA is an important marker for these diseases, and detection of UA is highly important. In recent years, a plethora of approaches have been developed for determination of UA, including electrochemical methods [4,5] {Sun, 2011 #29;Yu, 2016 #30}, chemiluminescence [6,7], colorimetry [8,9] and chromatography [10]. Compared with these methods, the fluorescence method has great advantages, including low cost, convenience, rapidity and high sensitivity. Most reported methods for detecting UA are generally based on detecting the fluorescence intensity at a single emission wavelength [11–14], but such methods are susceptible to the test instrument and the external environment. For the ratiometric method, it is based on the measurement of the intensity ratio of two different wavelengths. It has a self-calibration function, accurate and high sensitivity, and is also used for the determination of H2O2 and UA [15–17]. For example, Xiao et al. reported a ratiometric fluorescence based on oxOPD and B, N-CDs to detect H2O2 and UA, in which ZnFe2O4 magnetic microspheres with peroxidase activity catalyze H2O2 to oxidize o-phenylenediamine to produce oxOPD [15]. Meng et al. synthesized catalase-modified gold nanoclusters (CAT-GNCs) by one-step method in 15 min for the determination of H2O2 and UA, and CAT-GNCs have dual fluorescence emission [16]. Yang et al. designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on BSA-Au NCs and coumarin using Fenton effect. Fe2+ catalyzed H2O2 quenching BSA-Au NCs fluorescence and oxidized coumarin to produce fluorescence, achieving the purpose of detecting H2O2 and UA [17].
Metal nanoclusters (such as Au, Ag, Cu), as a new class of fluorophores, are widely used to construct biosensors due to their unique physical and optical properties. Metal nanoclusters possess a variety of advantages, such as simple and convenient synthesis, good light stability, large Stokes shift and low toxicity, thus they have potential applications in bioimaging, catalysis and medicine [18,19]{Wang, 2016 #83;He, 2018 #84}. Among the metal nanoclusters, Ag NCs are widely used in fluorescent biosensors. To date, various materials such as thiols, DNA polymers, polypeptides and proteins have been used to synthesize silver nanoclusters. Ag NCs have extensive application in the detection of metal ions [20–22], thiol molecules [23], DNA [24], protein [25], intracellular staining [26], etc. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) has a strong affinity for inorganic salts, thus it is used as stabilizer and capping agent in the process of synthesizing nanoparticles [27].
Based on the unique properties, hemin has been used in biomimetic catalysis for the synthesis of carbon dots. Because it is insoluble in water but soluble in acidic acetone or alkaline aqueous solution, branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) is beneficial to increase its solubility [28]. In order to increase its fluorescence intensity, citric acid can be used together with hemin and PEI as the carbon precursor [29]. The resulting N, Fe-CDs possess good fluorescent properties as well as peroxidase properties.
Herein, we report a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on BSA-Ag NCs and N, Fe-CDs doped carbon dots for the determination of UA. Firstly, H2O2 is produced through the catalytic oxidation of UA by uricase. Next, the carbon dots catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals that etch silver nanoclusters. The intensity of the red fluorescence emission of BSA-Ag NCs decreases, while the intensity of the blue emission of N, Fe-CDs increases. This method has good selectivity and high sensitivity with a low detection limit, and it was applied in the determination of UA in urine samples. This method can be used for sensitive and convenient determination of UA in biomedical applications.


### EXPERIMENTAL ###



### MATERIALS ###

The reagents of UA, UA oxidase, bovine serum albumin, H2O2, sodium hydroxide, silver nitrate, urea, creatinine, oxalic acid, ascorbic acid, citric acid, lactose, galactose, fructose, and sucrose, glucose, tyrosine, arginine, histidine, glutamic acid, glycine, alanine, phenylalanine, valine, serine, dopamine, glutathione, cysteine, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, UA oxidase and UA were obtained from Shanghai Ryon Biological Technology CO., Ltd.(Shanghai, China). Phosphotungstic acid kit was manufactured by from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). All reagents were analytically pure without further procession before use. PBS buffer (pH 7.2–7.4) used in the experiment was purchased from KeLong (Chengdu, China). Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18.25 MΩ cm) was used as solvent during the whole experiment.


### INSTRUMENTATION ###

Fluorescence spectroscopy experiments were performed on FL-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fixed excitation wavelength was 390 nm and the fluorescence spectra were in the range of 330–760 nm, with the excitation and emission slit widths of 10.0 nm and 20.0 nm, respectively. The scanning speed was 1200 nm/min with a response time of 2.0 s, and the PMT voltage was 610 V. UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) was used for collecting the absorption spectrum of BSA-Ag NCs. BSA-Ag NCs and N, Fe-CDs TEM images were collected with JEM 2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan). Hydrated particle size and surface charge were measured by ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK). The Escalab 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, US) was used to characterize the elemental composition and valence states.


### SYNTHESIS OF N, FE-CDS ###

The N, Fe-CDs was prepared based on a previous report with certain modifications [28]. Specifically, 2.0 g PEI was mixed in 10 mL deionized water, followed by the addition of 50.0 mg hemin and 20.0 mg trisodium citrate. They were mixed ultrasonically and heated for 10 h at 180 °C in an autoclave. Final product was preserved at 4 °C for further use.


### SYNTHESIS OF BSA-AG NCS ###

BSA-Ag NCs was prepared by a modified method according to a previous report [30]. 10.0 mL BSA solution (50 mg/mL) was placed at room temperature, then 10.0 mL AgNO3 (0.01 M) was slowly added to the BSA solution with vigorous stirring. After reacting for 30 min, 0.6 mL NaOH solution (1.0 M) was added to the solution with continuous stirring for another 30 min. Subsequently, 4.0 mL NaBH4 (0.01 M) was added and mixed well with continuous stirring for 2.5 h. Once the color of the BSA-Ag NCs solution turned to reddish brown, this synthesis was completed and the final solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiment.


### H2O2 DETERMINATION ###

Firstly, 10 μL 30% H2O2 and 100 μL N, Fe-CDs solution were diluted to 100 mL and 5 mL, respectively. Next, 50 μL diluted carbon dots solution was mixed with 400 μL BSA-Ag NCs, and H2O2 solution was added to reach an overall volume of 3 mL. After incubation for 35 min at 35 °C, fluorescence measurement was performed. Since H2O2 is easily decomposed, it is necessary to reconstitute the H2O2 solution once the concentration of H2O2 is appreciably reduced. The fluorescence emission spectrum was collected with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.


### UA DETECTION ###

UA oxidase (10 unit/mg) was dissolved in PBS buffer (pH 7.2–7.4) to prepare a urate oxidase solution, which was stored at −20 °C for subsequent use.
UA of different volumes was mixed into the enzyme solution with identical concentration, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Afterwards, the N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs were added to the mixture, and the resulting mixture was kept at 35 °C for 35 min with a final volume of 3 mL. Subsequent experimental steps are the same as above.


### DETERMINATION OF UA IN HUMAN URINE ###

Adult male and female’s urine samples were collected to assess the performance of the proposed sensing system, which were incubated in water at 50 °C for further use. The measurement conditions and experimental procedures are the same as above. The concentration of UA is derived from the I450/I650 on the working curve. The pretreated samples were detected according to the proposed method. The recovery of the spiked sample was then measured. The concentrations of UA in urine samples were calculated from the linear equation.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###



### FEASIBILITY AND PRINCIPLE OF RATIOMETRIC FLUORESCENCE ###

To test the feasibility of the experiment, the emission fluorescence of N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs were firstly measured. The fluorescence emission of carbon dots and silver clusters were at 450 nm and 650 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A). By fixing the amount of N, Fe-CDs and changing the concentration of silver clusters (Fig. 1B), it can be observed that as the concentration of the silver clusters increases, the fluorescence emission intensity of the N, Fe-CDs decreases, and the fluorescence intensity of BSA-Ag NCs increases. Then the optimal excitation wavelength of the ratio fluorescent sensing system was measured. With the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, the emission peak shape of the carbon dots was better, and the fluorescence ratio of the carbon dots and the silver clusters was the lowest (Fig. 1C). As shown in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence of N, Fe-CDs did not significantly change after mixing with UA and uricase, respectively. However, with the addition of UA and uricase at the same time, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-Ag NCs decreased, while the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots significantly recovered. These results demonstrate that the present ratiometric fluorescent sensing system is feasible.
Silver clusters can be etched by hydroxyl radicals to quench the fluorescence of silver clusters. Up to now, many reports have shown that H2O2 can quench the fluorescence of metal nanoclusters [31–33]. When H2O2 is catalyzed into hydroxyl radicals, the oxidizing ability of hydroxyl radicals is much stronger than that of H2O2, and hydroxyl radicals can quench metal nanoclusters more efficiently [17,34]. Based on this, a new fluorescence analysis method is established in this study for determine of UA. The principle for this sensing system is illustrated in Scheme 1 . In the presence of oxygen, UA is catalyzed by uricase to produce H2O2 and allantoin. N, Fe-CDs possesses fluorescent properties as well as peroxidase activity, and it can catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals to etch silver clusters, thereby effectively quenching the fluorescence of silver clusters. The combination of the fluorescent emission from silver clusters and carbon dots forms a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system. Thus, using H2O2 as an intermediary, a UA biosensor was constructed to achieve quantitative determination.


### PEROXIDASE ACTIVITY OF N, FE-CDS ###

N, Fe-CDs act similarly to Fenton's reagents, and it has peroxidase activity that catalyzes the production of hydroxyl radicals by H2O2 [28]. In order to study the catalytic activity of N, Fe-CD, different concentrations of H2O2 were added in the presence or absence of N, Fe-CDs (Fig. S1). It was found that in the presence of N, Fe-CDs, as the concentration of H2O2 increased, the color of TMB changed from colorless to blue, and it had an absorption peak at 652 nm (Fig. S1 A). When N, Fe-CDs are absent, as the concentration of H2O2 increases, TMB remains colorless and will not be oxidized (Fig. S1B). In addition, other typical peroxidase substrates were tested in the presence of H2O2 and N, Fe-CDs. It can be seen from Fig. S2 that N, Fe-CDs catalyze H2O2 to produce OH radicals to deoxidize ABTS and OPD, the color of OPD turns yellow and ABTS turns green.


### CHARACTERIZATION OF THE N, FE-CDS ###

To characterize the morphology and size of the carbon dots, HRTEM and DLS data were collected. Fig. 2 A shows that the carbon dots are mono-dispersed with spherical or spheroidal morphology. The average diameter is approximately 10.7 nm (Fig. 2B). Zeta potential of the surface of the carbon dots is -9.54 mV (Fig. 2C), indicating that the N, Fe-CDs has a negative charge. which can be caused by partial ionization of the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the carbon dots to form oxygen anions.
It is essential to learn the surface composition and chemical state of N, Fe-CDs, which can be characterized by XPS analysis. The full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 A) shows that the elements of C, N, O and Fe are present in the carbon dots. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s displays three component peaks at 284.7, 286.1 and 288.1 eV, due to the CC, CO/CN and CO, COOH bonds, respectively (Fig. 3B). The N1s spectrum of the carbon dots (Fig. 3C) shows three peaks at 399.1, 400.6 and 401.7 eV corresponding to the CNC, N(C)3 and NH bonds, respectively. The peak energy of O 1 s is approximately 532.1 eV ( Fig. 3D), corresponding to the CO/CO bonds. The binding energy of the high resolution XPS spectrum at 711.8, 724.4 eV satellite peak confirmed the presence of Fe (Fig. 3E). [28,35]


### CHARACTERIZATION OF THE BSA-AG NCS ###

In order to investigate the morphology, chemical structure and composition of BSA-Ag NCs, HRTEM, zeta potential and XPS data were collected. From the TEM image, the silver clusters are well dispersed without obvious aggregation, which clearly shows the high crystallinity of BSA-Ag NCs with a lattice parameter of 0.14 nm. The surface zeta potential is -28.5 mV, indicating that BSA-Ag NCs are negatively charged. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S3 A) shows that the binding energies correspond to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S3B) shows peaks of 284.8, 286.2, 288.1 and 289.6 eV, corresponding to CC, CO/CN and CO, COOH bonds, respectively [36]. The N1s spectrum is mainly the CN bond corresponding to 399.9 eV (Fig. S3C). As shown in Fig. S3D, the spectrum of O 1 s correspond to CO/CO. Besides, a weak peak at a binding energy was observed due to Ag 3d. As shown in Fig. S3E, for the Ag 3d XPS spectrum, the satellite peaks of 368.1 and 374 eV in the high-resolution spectrum verified the existence of Ag0, indicating the successful synthesis of BSA-Ag NCs [37].


### OPTIMIZATION OF THE EXPERIMENTAL FACTORS ###

The experimental conditions were optimized using the intermediate of H2O2. The reaction temperature, time and stability were adjusted. As shown in Fig. S4, the higher temperature has a significant impact on the system. In the absence of H2O2, the temperature itself affects the fluorescence emission intensity of the sensing system. The ratio of I450/I650 is maximal at 35 °C. As for the time, it was observed that the system gradually became stable after 35 min, thus 35 °C and 35 min (Fig. S5) were selected as the optimum conditions for subsequent experiments. The ratiometric sensing system is barely affected by different concentrations of NaCl solution and pH (Fig. S6), which proves that the system is relatively stable.


### IFE BETWEEN N, FE-CDS AND BSA-AG NCS ###

To investigate the fluorescence quenching between the two fluorescence emission constituting the ratiometric fluorescence, the UV–vis spectrum and the fluorescence emission spectrum were collected. As exhibited in Fig. S7, the BSA-Ag NCs have a wide absorption band (380–700 nm), and the UV–vis spectrum of BSA-Ag NCs overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of N, Fe-CDs (450 nm). Thus, it is speculated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is either fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or internal filter effect (IFE). IFE refers to the concentration effect caused by the overlap of emission and absorption spectra of fluorescent substances [38]. To confirm the quenching mechanism, as shown in Fig. S8, the UV–vis spectrum of the BSA-Ag NCs solution was recorded in the absence and presence of N, Fe-CDs. In the presence and absence of N, Fe-CDs, the position and intensity of the absorption band of BSA-Ag NCs remains essentially the same, indicating that no complex was formed and FRET did not occur between BSA-Ag NCs and N, Fe-CDs. In order to further verify the mechanism of quenching due to IFE, the zeta potential was measured. The zeta potentials of N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs were -9.54 mV (Fig. 2C) and -28.50 mV, respectively (Fig. 4 B). N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs all have negative charge, thus there is no electrostatic interaction between them. N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs will not stick together, and the distance between them should be more than 10 nm [39]. It is speculated that the quenching mechanism of N, Fe-CDs/BSA-Ag NCs double-emitting fluorescent probes is IFE rather than FRET [15]. Therefore, fluorescence resonance energy transfer is unlikely to occur between the carbon dots and the silver clusters, and the fluorescence change between N, Fe-CDs and BSA-Ag NCs is attributed to IFE.


### CALIBRATION CURVES AND DETECTION LIMIT ###

Prior to the determination of UA, the N, Fe-CDs/BSA-Ag NCs ratiometric fluorescence was used to determine H2O2, which is an intermediate product obtained by uricase catalyzing UA. Under optimal experimental conditions, as the concentration of H2O2 increases, the fluorescent emission intensity of BSA-Ag NCs at 650 nm decreases, but the fluorescent emission of N, Fe-CDs at 450 nm increases (Fig. 5 A). The linear range of H2O2 was 0.6–100 μM with a good linear relationship with the fluorescent emission ratio (Fig. 5B). The linear equation of H2O2 is I450/I650 = 0.03994CH2O2 + 0.55863 (CH2O2: μM), and the detection limit is 0.30 μM (R2 = 0.99296) (S/N = 3). F represents the fluorescence emission ratio of N, Fe-CDs/BSA-Ag NCs in the system, and C is the concentration of H2O2. These results show that N, Fe-CDs/BSA-Ag NCs are sensitive to the fluorescence response of H2O2. This method is comparable to previous reports on determination of H2O2 by fluorescence (Table. S1).
The response of the sensing system to UA was determined under optimized experimental conditions. It is observed in Fig. 6 A, with increasing UA concentration, the intensity of fluorescence emission from BSA-Ag NCs decreases, while the intensity of the fluorescence emission from N, Fe-CDs increases. With the concentration range of UA between 0.8 and 133 μM, there is a good linear relationship between intensity ratio and UA concentration, with a detection limit of 0.49 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 6B). The corresponding linear equation is as follow: I450/I650 = 0.02439CUA + 0.52866 (CUA: μM, R2 = 0.99106). As shown in Fig. 6B, F is the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the composite sensor in the system and C refers to the concentration of UA. As shown in Table 1 , this sensing system has high sensitivity for the determination of UA. Compared with other literature reports on UA’s detection, this method has significant advantages (Table S2).


### THE SELECTIVITY ###

The selectivity of the present method was studied against the interfering substances (Fig. 7 ) including ascorbic acid, citric acid, dopamine, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, oxalic acid, glucose, lactose, sucrose, fructose, galactose, urea, alanine, glycine, glutamic acid, arginine, tyrosine, histidine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, cysteine and glutathione. The concentration of inorganic salt ions and urea is 100 times that of UA, the concentration of amino acids and other substances is 10 times that of UA. Glutathione and cysteine interfere with the system, but their content in urine is much lower than that of UA [40], so they take half of the UA concentration. The I450/I650 ratio of the final detected interfering substances is far below the UA, so the degree of change in the fluorescence intensity of the system caused by these interfering substances is negligible compared with UA. Thus, this method has reasonably high selectivity.


### DETECTION OF UA IN HUMAN URINE ###

To evaluate the practicability of this method, human urine samples were collected to detect the UA content. The test results of the sensing system were compared with the phosphotungstic acid kit. The precision and accuracy of this sensing system was assessed by determination of the recovery of spiked samples. As illustrated in Table 1, the results from the present sensing system were consistent with those obtained by the phosphotungstic acid kit. The recovery is 96.17%–104.58% and the RSD is 2.38%–5.27%. Hence, the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor is suitable for the determination of UA content in human urine. This method has great potential for application in analysis of UA in real blood samples, including clinical blood samples.


### CONCLUSION ###

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence determination method for UA based on BSA-Ag NCs and N, Fe-CDs was constructed. In this sensing system, uricase catalyzes UA to produce H2O2, and the fluorescent carbon dots with peroxidase activity were used to catalyze H2O2, generating hydroxyl radicals to etch silver clusters and achieving the purpose of detecting UA. This method delivers a broad linear range of 0.8–133 μM and a low detection limit of 0.49 μM (S/N = 3). This method exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity under various conditions, and has been applied to determine H2O2 and UA. It is expected that this method can be widely applied to the detection of various compounds involving H2O2 as the intermediate.
