### INTRODUCTION ###

Fluorescent materials have been widely used for chem- and bio-sensing [1], bio-imaging [2], photoelectric conversion [3], photocatalysis [4], photodynamic therapy [5], whitening of paper and fabric [6], improving the vividness of color coatings [7] and enhancing the efficiency of sunlight utilization through incorporation of fluorescent materials in agricultural films [8]. Numerous fluorescent dyes and nanomaterials have been prepared to meet the requirements of various applications, which include small molecule organic dyes, organometallic complexes, polymer-based fluorescent materials, inorganic quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots and metallic nanocrystals. However, with the rapid increase of their applications, their safety has become a serious concern. Most of fluorescent materials are not easy to be degraded, their accumulation in environment may cause detrimental effects to the ecosystem, endangering human health through food chain [9]. Lots of traditional organic dyes possess conjugated structures (such as phenyl, naphthyl, oxazolyl, thiophene and other groups), may have potential carcinogenic risks. Inorganic QDs, such as CdTe, CdS and CdSe, may release heavy metal ions and cause acute toxicity to organisms [10]. Many ways have been proposed to eliminate or reduce the potential toxicity. Modification or coating on the toxic fluorescent materials with bio-compatible layers is a frequently adopted method. It is effective to coat semiconductor QDs with shells to prevent the leakage of the heavy metals and reduce their toxicity [11]. However, these operations are rather complicated, normally need multiple steps, significantly increase the cost for final products. Preparation of fluorescent materials with low toxicity and good performance is still a major task. Fluorescent carbon dots have been proven to be a good alternative, but their compositions are still unclear, their long term safety may need further investigation [12,13]. Based on this fact, exploring fluorescent substances from safe resources can be an alternative route for safer fluorescence application.
Caramel is an important dietary material produced and consumed widely for coloring and flavoring of food and beverage all over the world for a long time [14]. It is normally obtained through heating of sugars with or without additives [14,15]. As a commercialized product with a history of over 150 years, its safety as a food additive has undergone strict scrutiny. Studies on its potential effect on toxicokinetics, genotoxicity, subchronic toxicity, carcinogenicity and reproductive/developmental toxicity have been conducted [16], potential carcinogen impurities such as 4-methylimadazole (4-MeI) and 2-Acetyl-4-tetrahydroxy-butylimidazole (THI) are strictly regulated either in US or other countries [17]. Therefore, caramel can be a safe reagent if it can be used for sensing purpose. Although it was known that caramel is fluorescent, and quantitative analyses of caramel with fluorescence methods have been reported [18–20], application of caramel as fluorescent reagent is seldom found, possibly because of its complicated composition. Actually there are consistent efforts to unravel the composition of caramel [21,22], but as mentioned by Golon et al. [21], lack of suitable analytical techniques with sufficient resolution power for this complicated mixture is the main obstacle. According to Golon et al. [21], caramel contains oligomers with up to six carbohydrate units in forms of dehydration products, hydration products, disproportionation products and colored aromatic substances. There is large opportunity to have fluorescent components in this complicated cocktail.
Nitrophenols are listed as priority pollutants by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [23]. Among them, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) are widely used for manufacturing of explosives, fireworks, matches and rocket fuels [24]. Nitrophenols such as 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), DNP and TNP also act as the raw materials in industries of leathers, dyes and medicines [25,26]. Due to their widespread applications, it is easy to be released into groundwater and soil, may cause stimulation to the skin and eyes and even respiratory damage of whom comes into contact occasionally [27,28]. Some researches show that TNP and its metabolites picramic acid are carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic [29]. Due to its strong electron withdrawing ability, it is difficult to be degraded into non-toxic substances [30]. Therefore, the effective monitoring and detection of trace TNP, DNP and 4-NP in the environment are very important. So far, many methods have been used for the detection of nitrophenols, such as mass spectrometry [31], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [32], X-ray imaging [33], ELISA [34], but these techniques normally need expensive equipment, professional operation and tedious procedures. Electrochemistry can be used for the detection of such nitroaromatic explosives, but its selectivity and robustness may need further improvement [35]. Comparing with these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted intensive attention due to its advantages including high sensitivity, rapid detection speed and convenient operation. A group of functionalized fluorescent materials have been used for the detection of nitrophenols, which include organic dyes [36–38], metal nanoclusters [39], conjugated polymers [40], carbon nanomaterials [41] and quantum dots [42]. However, potential biotoxicity of these materials together with other difficulties in material preparation and stability in aqueous solution still implies the necessity of non-toxic and easily available materials for rapid analysis of nitrophenols.
Herein, a commercially available caramel was simply separated into two portions through dialysis. The dialysate with low molecular weight (low MW) exhibited bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of UV light and the retentate with high molecular weight (high MW) showed dark typical color of caramel but it is barely fluorescent. The dialysate was systematically characterized to deduce the possible origin of fluorescence. The fluorescent dialysate was successfully used for the rapid and sensitive detection of TNP, DNP and 4-NP. The ratios of nitrophenols can be estimated by controlling the pH value during the assay. The dialysed caramel was successfully used for water and soil samples, which demonstrates its potential as a low-cost, non-toxic fluorescent reagent.


### MATERIAL AND METHODS ###



### REAGENTS AND MATERIALS ###

Caramel was purchased from Apple Flavor & Fragrance Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China, complying with the standard GB 1886.64–2015). Dialysis membrane tubes with MW cutoff of 8000–14400 Da (Wordbio Co. Ltd., Xi’an, China) were used. Nitrobenzene (NB), 1-Chloro-4-nitrobenzene (ClNB), 1,3-dinitrobenzene (DNB), 1-Chloro-dinitrobenzene (ClDNB), methylbenzene (MB), 4-nitrotoluene (NT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), benzoic acid (BA), 2-nitrobenzoic acid (NBA), 4-Chlorobenzoicacid (ClBA), 3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid (DNBA), aniline (AN), phenol (PHE), 2-aminophenol (AP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) was obtained from Xilong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA)) was used in all experiments.
Warning! TNT and TNP, are highly explosive, should be handled carefully in small quantities with appropriate safety measures.


### INSTRUMENTATION AND CHARACTERIZATION ###

Elemental analysis was carried out using a vario EL cube Elemental Analyzer (elementar, Langenselbold, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer (Madison, WI) using KBr pellets. 1H Nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on a JNM-ECS (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan, 400 MHz). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (Eindhoven, The Netherlands) operating at 200 kV. Hydrodynamic diameters of the fluorescent dialysate were measured by a dynamic light scattering instrument (DLS, BI-200SM, Brookhaven Instruments Corp., Holtsville, New York). The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained on an X′Pert diffractometer (Philips, Almelo, The Netherlands). The fluorescence spectra were measured on an RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) with both excitation and emission slits set at 10 nm and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV 2800SPC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Sunny Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system combined with an FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, West Lothian, U.K.).


### DIALYSIS OF CARAMEL ###

To completely separate the fluorescent components, aqueous solution of caramel (100 mg/mL) of 5 mL was loaded into a dialysis tube (MW 8000–14400 Da), the tube was clamped and soaked in 2 L ultrapure water. The dialysate was collected after 24 h and fresh water (2 L) was replenished for another cycle of dialysis for 24 h. The process was repeated (3 times) until the dialysate was not fluorescent (checked with a 405 nm laser pointer). All dialysates were combined and concentrated using a rotary evaporator to a volume of 20 mL. Finally, the fluorescent components were freeze-dried for further use. To get dialysed caramel for the detection, 1 cycle of dialysis for 4–6 h was normally adopted.


### PRETREATMENT OF REAL SAMPLES ###

Water samples collected from the Yellow River and a eutrophic pond were filtered through 0.22 µm filters before analysis.
Three kinds of soil samples (loess, the Yellow River sediments and street dust) were collected from the campus of Lanzhou University, the bank of Yellow River and Donggang Road (Lanzhou, China), respectively. The soil samples were treated using a previously reported protocol with slight modification [43]. First, the soil was naturally dried and grinded with a mortar and pestle. Then 1.0 g of sample was extracted with 4.00 mL acetone through ultrasonication for 15 min and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was collected and dried under nitrogen. The residual was dissolved in 2.5 mL pure water for analysis. Each sample was measured under pH 3, 5 and 8.


### FLUORESCENCE DETECTION OF TNP, DNP AND 4-NP ###

Three aliquots of 2.5 mL nitrophenols (NPs) solution of various concentrations were added separately into 0.5 mL of dialysed caramel (420 μg/mL) in McIlvaine's buffer at pH 3, 5 and 8. The fluorescence intensities were measured at λex/λem of 350/434 nm, and ΔF (ΔF = F 0 − F) values were calculated, where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of the dialysed caramel mixed with 2.5 mL pure water and F is that with NPs. The calibration curves were prepared by plotting F 0/F − 1 versus NP concentrations. All measurements were performed at least thrice at room temperature.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###



### FLUORESCENT CARAMEL ###



### DIALYSIS OF RAW CARAMEL ###

Caramel used in this work is produced by heating sugars in the presence of ammoniate. It is reasonable to have fluorescent components after a series of condensation, dehydration, Maillard reaction and carbonization [44]. However, commercially available caramel is weakly fluorescent, partially because of its dark color and self-quenching. To remove the dark color, dialysis was utilized to separate the components based on its size. It was observed that components of low MW are highly fluorescent, a dialysis membrane with a MW cutoff of 8000–14400 Da could well separate the components into a portion of fluorescent dialysate and a portion of non-fluorescent retentate ( Fig. 1). The dialysis was continued with repetitive water change every 24 h until no fluorescent components came out. All dialysates were combined and dried to get pale yellow powder, which is 60% (w/w) of original caramel. The other 40% possesses typical dark color of caramel but it is barely fluorescent. It should be mentioned that multiple dialysis steps with membranes of different MW cutoff were employed for the characterizing purpose, to be used as a fluorescent reagent, one step of dialysis is sufficient.


### OPTICAL PROPERTIES AND STRUCTURAL CHARACTERIZATION ###

The optical properties of the fluorescent dialysate were examined by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometry. UV–Vis spectrum shows a strong characteristic absorption peak at 270 nm ( Fig. 2a), corresponding to π-π* electron transition of the conjugated C˭C bonds and a broad absorption peak between 300 and 400 nm, which can be attributed to n-π* electron transition of the C˭O bonds [45]. As shown in the fluorescence spectra, the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of the dialysate are 350 nm and 434 nm, respectively (Fig. 2a). Under the 365 nm UV lamp, bright blue fluorescence was observed. With quinine sulfate as the reference, the measured quantum yield of dialysed caramel was 6.5%. Fig. 2b is the emission spectra obtained at excitation from 310 to 400 nm range, it is obvious that emission is excitation wavelength dependent, which indicates the multiple emission mechanisms. The bathochromic-shift is clear in the normalized spectra (Fig. S1). Based on these phenomena we presumed that the fluorescence emission is produced by randomly distributed 3-dimensionally conjugated structure.
Elemental analysis results of the dialysed caramel (C, 47.4; H, 4.6; N, 3.6; O, 44.4%) represent a formula of C31H36N2O22, roughly corresponds to a loss of 1.8 molecule of H2O per hexose unit. Based on the O/C and H/C ratios, the fluorescent components still belong to dehydration products of sugar oligomers according to Golon et al. [21]. This speculation is also confirmed by the FTIR spectrum. The absorption peak at 3426 cm−1 indicates N-H and O-H stretching vibration, the absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 indicates the C˭O (in amide or carboxyl group) or C˭C stretching vibration, the absorption peak at 1409 cm−1 is caused by the deformation vibration of C-H, both 1202 and 1045 cm−1 point to C-O stretching vibration ( Fig. 3a), these peaks indicate the presence of hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl and ether functional groups.
The difference between of the fluorescent and dark colored portions was characterized by 1H NMR. Caramel (Fig. S2) and its fluorescent portion (Fig. 3b blue line) show the similar 1H NMR spectra, with peaks at 0.7–4.0 ppm (sp3 C-H protons and the protons attached with hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups), 4.0–5.4 ppm (HC˭CH protons), 6.2–8.7 ppm (aromatic protons) and 9.3 ppm (the protons associated with aldehyde group). By contrast, the 1H NMR spectrum of the dark colored portion (Fig. 3b black line) is mainly composed of several broad peaks in the corresponding positions and is absence of the protons from aldehyde group at 9.3 ppm, which reveals high degree of polymerization and crosslinking in the dark colored portion.
TEM indicates the presence of two kinds of nanoparticles with different sizes in the dialysed caramel ( Fig. 4a). The size of the larger ones ranges from 7.9 to 12.1 nm (Fig. S3a), while the smaller ones in a range of 1.6–4.4 nm (Fig. S3b). Meanwhile, average hydrodynamic diameter of the dialysed caramel is determined as 4.2 nm by DLS result (Fig. S4), which implies that the larger nanoparticles were probably formed by the aggregation of small nanoparticles during the process of drying [46]. These images are similar to the carbon dots reported previously, but we didn't refer to the dialysed components as carbon dots because it is found that dialysate obtained with a membrane of cutoff MW as small as 500 Da is still highly fluorescent. It is unreasonable to draw a conclusion that the fluorescence is originated from these nanoparticles, just as mentioned by Essner et al. [47]. On the other hand, since about 60% of caramel is fluorescent and the carbonized portion (colored components) is not fluorescent, caramel is apparently a more pertinent name based on the conventional nomenclature. XRD pattern (Fig. 4b) showed a broad peak at 23°, which indicates an amorphous structure, consistent with the random condensation and dehydration of carbohydrate units.


### STABILITY OF DIALYSED CARAMEL ###

Influence of external conditions on the fluorescence intensity of the dialysed caramel was also investigated. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescence intensities under different pH conditions, fluorescence emission is stable at pHs from 4 to 10. It decreased in strong acidic and basic medium, which demonstrates that protonation or deprotonation may change the structure and electron transition [48]. This behavior is consistent with the fact that there are amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the dialysed caramel as discussed above. Ionic strength showed no influence on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5), which demonstrates that electrostatic interaction plays a very limited role in the fluorescence emission. This is good for situations where high concentration of salts have to be used, for example, PBS buffers used in most of biological applications. The fluorescence was stable after continuous illumination for 1 h, which proves suitability for long time continuous analysis (Fig. 5b).


### SENSING OF TNP, DNP AND 4-NP WITH DIALYSED CARAMEL ###



### DETECTION MECHANISM ###

As shown in Fig. 6a, b and c, the fluorescence intensity of the dialysed caramel decreased evidently with the increase of the concentration of TNP, DNP or 4-NP, the fluorescence emission peak was also slightly red-shifted. To elucidate the possible mechanism, fluorescence spectra of the dialysed caramel and UV–Vis spectra of NPs were taken. As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorption band of NPs are overlapped with the excitation and emission bands of the dialysed caramel, suggesting the possibility of the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [49,50]. But the fluorescence lifetime of the dialysed caramel was basically unchanged after the addition of TNP, which eliminates the possibility of FRET during the quenching process (Fig. 7b). In addition, the attenuation of fluorescence of dialysed caramel is proportional to NPs' absorbance at 350 nm (the maximum excitation wavelength of dialysed caramel), rather than the overlapped area of the absorption band and the excitation /emission peaks. This is also an evidence of IFE [51].
NP exhibits strong absorption in the range of 300–460 nm at pH higher than its pKa, where it converts to phenoxide. The pKa of 4-NP, DNP and TNP is 7.1, 4.2 and 0.38, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8, fluorescence quenching efficiencies by NPs at different pHs are quite different, providing a possibility to resolve them through the measurements with different acidity [51].


### SIMULTANEOUS DETECTION OF THREE NPS ###

The linear relationships between concentration of NPs and F 0 /F − 1 at different pH values were described in Fig. 9. The content of each nitrophenol in a sample with mixed NPs can be calculated by solving the following equation obtained at three different pHs (pH 3, 5 and 8): (1) K TNP ⋅ C TNP + K DNP ⋅ C DNP + K 4 − NP ⋅ C 4 − NP = F 0 / F − 1 where C represents the concentration of corresponding NP, K is the slope of the calibration curve obtained at each pH. The equations with real K values are given in Supporting information.


### ANALYTICAL PERFORMANCE ###

The detection range and limit of detection (LOD) of NPs were summarized in Table S1, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for the measurement of 5 µM NPs in the range of 0.9–3.6% (intra-day, n = 3) and 1.7–4.2% (inter-day, n = 3). The linear range of TNP is 0.2–22 μM with an LOD of 0.09 µM, which is superior to or comparable with those of fluorimetric methods reported recently using various materials (Table S2), but the advantage with regard to the cost of dialysed caramel is evident.
To evaluate its selectivity for NPs, metal ions may co-exist in the normal water samples, which include Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and organic molecules with similar structures to NPs, including NB, DNB, ClNB, ClDNB, MB, NT, DNT, TNT, BA, NBA, ClBA, DNBA, AN, PHE, AP, 2-NP and 3-NP, were tested under the same condition. The fluorescent components show very good selectivity, as shown in Fig. 10a, only Fe3+ showed slight interference on the detection of NPs, because of its electrophilic nature and its color, but it can be readily eliminated by addition of reducing reagent, ascorbic acid (AA). AA itself does not interfere the analysis, the presence of 1 mM AA causes virtually no change in the fluorescence intensity of dialysed caramel. The low quenching efficiencies toward 2-NP and 3-NPs are due to their much lower extinction coefficients than 4-NP [51]. The selectivity of dialysed components for NPs among other nitroaromatic substances is surprisingly good (Fig. 10b), which may provide a choice to quantitatively detect NPs among other nitro compounds.


### DETECTION OF NPS IN REAL SAMPLES ###

The Stern-Volmer equations for the mixed NPs at pH 3, 5 and 8 listed in the Supporting information were used to determine each NP in the real samples. As shown in Table 1, none of NPs was found in the Yellow River water, eutrophic pond water, loess, Yellow River sediments or street dust, which is reasonable due to the strict control of pollution. Mixed NPs were spiked at different concentrations, each of NP was reliably detected with recoveries in a range of 95–108%. RSD (n = 3) are less than 5.9%. These results indicate that dialysed caramel can be used in real analysis. The lower limits of quantitation of the proposed method (Table S1) is comparable to the method of EPA for the determination of nitrophenols in water and soil/sediment samples by GC/MS (50 µg/L and 3300 µg/kg for 4-NP/DNP) [52]. The sensitivity can be further improved through extraction or other preconcentration techniques. It should be mentioned that a sample pretreatment including extraction of the NPs with acetone, removal of organic solvent and re-dissolving the analytes with water, is needed for soil samples, which makes the method a little complicated. Further optimization of the process can be a remedy of this flaw.


### CONCLUSIONS ###

It is proven that caramel can be an efficient and low-cost fluorescent reagent for the simultaneous detection of TNP, DNP and 4-NP. Through a simple dialysis, about 60% of highly fluorescent components can be isolated from commercial caramel products. Due to the strict regulation of the hazardous impurities of caramel, the isolated components should be safe and environment compatible. The fluorescent components belong to dehydration products of carbohydrate oligomers and the dark colored components show high degree of polymerization and crosslinking. The fluorescent components were successfully used for resolving TNP, DNP and 4-NP by adjusting the pH values and the sensing mechanism is most probably IFE. Due to the wide availability of UV LED of 365 nm, this actually provides a way for the detection of NPs on-site without the concern of possible threaten from the fluorescence reagents. Their application in biological sensing is also expected.

### TITLE ###

Investigation of photo-induced electron transfer between amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots and selenium nanoparticle and it's application for sensitive fluorescent detection of copper ions


### AUTHOR GROUP ###

Yi Li
Weilu Zhang
Xiangmei Jiang
Yanxia Kou
Jiajia Lu
Liang Tan


### ABSTRACT ###

Copper ions play an essential role in some biological processes. Currently, there is a need for the development of convenient and reliable analytical methods for the Cu2+ measurement. In the present work, a sensitive fluorescence method was developed for the determination of copper ions. Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) and selenium nanoparticles (Se NPs) were synthetized, respectively, and they were characterized via transmission electron microscope, infrared spectrum analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectrum measurement. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between the prepared two nanomaterials could effectively quench the fluorescence of af-GQDs. Cu(II) was reduced to Cu(I) in the presence ascorbic acid and Cu2Se was finally generated on Se NPs surface, which led to the declined PET efficiency and inhibited the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs. The change in fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated to the logarithm of the Cu2+ concentration from 1 nM to 10 μM, with a detection limit of 0.4 nM under the optimal conditions. The detections of copper ions in water samples were realized via standard addition method and the recovery values varied from 98.7% to 103%. The proposed fluorescence method was also employed to analyze the uptake of Cu2+ into human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells and cisplatin-resistant HeLa cells (HeLa/DDP cells). The experimental results indicate that the decreased hCTR1 expression level in HeLa/DDP cells weakened the uptake of copper ions into these drug-resistant tumor cells.

### KEYWORDS ###

Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Selenium nanoparticles
Photo-induced electron transfer
Fluorescence quenching
Copper ions
Cellular uptake


### INTRODUCTION ###

As everyone knows, optimal uptake of trace elements is necessary for maintenance of homeostasis and improvement of cell protection [1]. Copper ions are important to numerous biological processes. For example, Cu2+ is a cofactor for many enzymes, including Cu, Zn superoxide dismutase (Cu, Zn SOD), lysyl oxidase, cytochrome c oxidase, and dopamine β-hydroxylase [2]. The excessive accumulation of Cu2+ in organisms is highly toxic because this heavy metal is non-degradable and therefore persistent. Disruption of copper homeostasis at the cellular level is associated with serious diseases, including cancer, Menkes syndrome, Alzheimer's and Wilson's diseases [3]. Not a few methods for Cu detection have been proposed, for example, ion chromatography [4], electrochemical measurement [5], fluorescence spectrometry [6], UV/vis spectrophotometry [7], chemiluminescence analysis [8], surface plasmon resonance [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10], atomic absorption spectroscopy [11], etc. Among these, fluorescence analysis has received great attention owing to facile operation, high sensitivity, good accuracy and stability.
Semiconductor nanoparticles with three dimensions confined to 2–8 nm length scale are usually called as quantum dots (QDs). They exhibit some unique optical properties including size-dependent tunable photoluminescence, high quantum yields, excellent photostability, narrow emission spectra and long fluorescence lifetimes [12–15]. QDs have attracted increasing attention in the past decade and they found their wide application on the fluorescent analysis of inorganic anions and cations, drugs, biomolecules, and so on. In recent years, a class of novel luminescent nanomaterials that is also named as QDs is graphene quantum dots (GQDs). It is well known that graphene is a kind of sp2 hybridized two dimensional carbon material with honeycomb network structure and it exhibits poor optical property due to the absence of band gap. However, GQDs, which are planar nanocrystals of graphene fragments, display superior photoluminescence performance [16,17]. The quantum yield of GQDs can be further enhanced once this material is functionalized at the surface and edge or doped with heteroatoms such as boron, nitrogen fluorine, sulfur and chlorine. Recent studies show that amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have attracted a great deal of interest as excellent fluorescent probes. The detected objects include metal ions [18], amino acid [19], DNA [20], enzyme [21], tumor marker [22], bacteria [23], and virus [24].
Selenium is well known for its semiconductor property, physiological function and pharmacological action including antioxidant, antitumor and immunoregulation. Nanomaterials display structural characteristics that are not available from bulk solids or molecules. It is reported that selenium nanoparticles (Se NPs) exhibit some prominent advantages such as chemical stability and biological activity because of their nanoscale size [25]. Se NPs have attracted considerable attention as a good nutritional supplement source of selenium element [26], and a possible candidate in clinical practice [27] although the high-dosage intake of Se NPs may induce chronic toxicity [28,29]. Some chemical and biologic analyses by using Se NPs have been reported. For example, Se NPs could be used as an alternative label for constructing lateral flow immunochromatographic strip detection of human chorionic gonadotropin, lipoprotein, and melamine [30]. A novel Se NPs-amplified chemiluminescence method has been applied for the determination of dinitrobutylphenol in water samples [31]. A disposable electrochemical paper-based sensor functionalized with Se NPs for the detection of both NO3 - and Hg2+ in water has been developed [32].
To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on the detection of Cu2+ ions via fluorescence assay by using af-GQDs and Se NPs simultaneously. In this work, these two nanomaterials were prepared, respectively, and transmission electron microscope (TEM), infrared spectroscopy (IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize them. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of af-GQDs by Se NPs was investigated. Sensitive fluorescent detection of Cu2+ was achieved based on the fluorescence intensity change of the af-GQDs-Se NPs system in the presence of copper ions and ascorbic acid. The proposed sensor was employed to measure the Cu2+ contents in water samples and investigate the uptake of copper ions into tumor cells and drug-resistant tumor cells.


### EXPERIMENTAL ###



### MATERIALS AND APPARATUS ###

All chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade. Selenious acid, ascorbic acid (vitamin C, Vc), sodium alginate, citric acid (CA), NaOH and copper sulphate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cisplatin (cis-diaminedichloroplatinum, DDP) was obtained from Jiangsu Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. China. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.1 M) and acetate buffer saline (ABS, 0.1 M) were prepared with the KH2PO4-Na2HPO4 mixture and the HAc-NaAc system, respectively. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.
An Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, equipped with a 300-μL micro quartz cell (1 cm path length), was employed for fluorescence measurements. The particle sizes of af-GQDs and Se NPs were determined ex situ by a Tecnai G2 20ST transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA). A Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) was applied for the infrared spectral analysis, and XPS analysis was carried out with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). The absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The cell-modality was determined via an Eclipse Ti-S inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan), in which the bright-field mode, ×10 eyepiece lens and ×20 objective lens were used.


### PREPARATION OF AF-GQDS AND SE NPS ###

Af-GQDs were synthesized based on CA pyrolysis by using the three-step method. Firstly, 0.5 g CA was heated at 175 °C for 30 min in oil bath until the colorless solid changed gradually to the orange liquid, which suggests the generation of GQDs. Secondly, 7 mL of aqueous ammonia was added into the obtained liquid slowly under ultrasonic condition in order to prepare the homogeneous solution. Finally, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 via dialysis, using a 300 D dialysis bag. The yellow af-GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C prior to use.
Se NPs were prepared in aqueous solution containing sodium alginate, which served as the soft template. First, the appropriate amount of sodium alginate solution was mixed with the selenious acid solution, and the ascorbic acid solution was added dropwise. The reaction proceeded under continuous ultrasonication for 2 h. The final concentration of sodium alginate, selenious acid and ascorbic acid was 0.2%, 1 mM and 4 mM, respectively. To collect the precipitate, the solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was removed, and 5 mL of pH 7.4 PBS containing 0.1% sodium alginate was used to induce the re-dispersion of the precipitate. The required red Se NPs suspension was obtained and it was stored at 4 °C for more than 1 month without obvious sedimentation.


### MEASUREMENT PROCEDURE ###

To a 0.5-mL polyethylene centrifugal tube, 100 μL of Se NPs suspension, 10 μL of 30 μM ascorbic acid solution and 10 μL of Cu2+ solution with different concentrations were added sequentially, and the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 150 min. Next, 30 μL of af-GQDs suspension were added into the mixture, and the volume of the solution was adjusted to 300 μL with 0.1 M PBS or ABS. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range from 380 to 650 nm at the excitation wavelength of 345 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm.


### CELL CULTURE AND ANALYSIS ###

Human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were obtained from XiangYa Central Laboratory of Central South University, China. RPMI1640 (Gibco) growth medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (Gibco) was used for the cell culture experiments. Cisplatin-resistant HeLa cells (HeLa/DDP cells) were obtained by incubating HeLa cells in the growth medium containing DDP. Initially 1 ng mL−1 DDP was introduced to HeLa cells and the content of the drug was gradually increased once HeLa cells matched their surroundings. As a result, HeLa/DDP cells could grow steadily in the presence of 0.2 μg mL−1 DDP. When the adherent HeLa cells or HeLa/DDP cells formed a dense monolayer on the substrate, they were trypsinized and separated from the growth medium by centrifugation. To remove adsorbed biomolecules, the cells were gently washed two times with sterile pH 7.4 PBS. They were re-suspended in pH 7.4 PBS at a concentration of 5 × 104 cells mL−1. Next, Cu2+ solution was mixed with the HeLa cells and HeLa/DDP cells suspension, respectively, and the final concentration of copper ions was 10 nM. Several hours later, the mixture composed of the tumor cells and Cu2+ was centrifuged. The supernatant was removed using a micropipette without disturbing the cell pellet. Finally, the Cu2+ content in the supernatant was investigated by using the above mentioned measurement procedure and the fluorescence method.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###



### CHARACTERIZATION OF AF-GQDS AND SE NPS ###

Fig. 1A shows the TEM image of the as-prepared af-GQDs. These quantum dots were well dispersed and their size was reasonably uniform with the average diameter of 5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the af-GQDs aqueous suspension was the transparent and light yellow solution. The functional groups of af-GQDs were investigated by FTIR analysis. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the absorption peaks at 3200 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of N–H bond, respectively. The absorption peak at 1406 cm−1 was derived from the bending vibration of C–N bond of amide group. The above results suggest the existence of amines at the surface and edge of graphene. The photoluminescence of af-GQDs can be improved due to the excellent electron-donating ability of amino groups [33]. The absorption peaks at 1706 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of C˭O and C–O bonds. They imply the presence of oxygen-containing groups in af-GQDs, which can ensure the good water solubility of the prepared quantum dots. In this study, sodium alginate was used in the preparation of Se NPs to regulate nanoparticle growth and prevent agglomeration. One can observe from the TEM image (Fig. 1D) that the Se suspension was comprised of monodispersed nanoparticles with a size distribution from 30 to 60 nm. As shown in Fig. 1E, the transparent and orange red solution was finally obtained after selenious acid was mixed with ascorbic acid. It means that monodinic selenium rather than trigonal selenium was generated because the latter is black [34]. As a surface-sensitive spectroscopic technique based on the photoelectric effect, XPS is normally used for the analysis of elemental composition. Each element can generate XPS peaks at characteristic binding energy values. The XPS analysis of Se NPs was performed and the result is shown in Fig. 1F. Two characteristic peaks at 55.9 eV and 55.1 eV corresponds to Se 3d was found, which are identical with the reported binding energies of elemental selenium [35,36]. So it can be concluded from Fig. 1D–F that elemental selenium nanoparticles were successfully prepared in the present work.


### FLUORESCENCE QUENCHING OF AF-GQDS INDUCED BY SE NPS AND INHIBITION EFFECT OF CU2+ ###

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of af-GQDs in PBS (pH 7.5) in the absence and presence of Se NPs, Cu2+, and Vc are shown in Fig. 2. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission peaks of af-GQDs were obtained at 345 nm and 443 nm, respectively (curves a′ and a). The fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs decreased considerably upon the addition of Se NPs (curves e′ and e). The UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs, Se NPs and their mixture were measured, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. A weak absorption peak at 324 nm was present on the spectrum of af-GQDs (curve a). Se NPs exhibited an obvious peak at about 264 nm (curve b). These characteristic absorbance peaks could be found on the spectrum of the mixture solution containing af-GQDs and Se NPs (curve c). The absorbance of af-GQDs derived from curve a and that of Se NPs obtained from curve b at the same wavelength was added together and the sum was displayed as curve d. Curves c and d almost overlapped with each other, suggesting that the two kinds of nanoparticles could not form a complex in the ground state. Se NPs with different concentration were added into the af-GQDs solution (in pH 7.5 PBS), and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. One can find from Fig. 4 that the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with increasing Se NP concentration and the emission peak exhibited a dramatic red shift. As shown in the insert, the quenching response was fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, (1) F 0 / F = 1 + K sv [ Se NPs ] where F 0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs before and after the addition of Se NPs, respectively. The quenching constant, K sv, was estimated to be 2.85 × 103 M−1. These experimental results suggest that dynamic quenching rather than static quenching led to the fluorescence intensity decrease of af-GQDs in the presence of Se NPs. In addition, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of af-GQDs hardly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Se NPs. It indicates that the fluorescence quenching was derived from neither inner filter effect nor fluorescence resonance energy transfer. It is known that carbon nanomaterials with delocalized π-electron system often act as the electron donor when they react with electron-withdrawing agents [37]. In the present work, photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from electron-rich af-GQDs to electron-deficient Se NPs produced non-radiative exciplex, (af-GQDs2+Se2-)*, and finally resulted in the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs according to Eq. (2). (2) * af - GQDs + Se 0 → * af - GQDs ⋯ Se 0 → ( af - GQDs 2 + Se 2 - ) * → af - GQDs 2 + + Se 2 -
It is observed from curves b′, b, c′, c, d′ and d in Fig. 2 that the fluorescence-intensity changes of af-GQDs were negligible in the presence of Cu2+, Vc and their mixture. The fluorescence-quenching extent of af-GQDs after the introduction of the combination of Se NPs and Vc (curves f′ and f) and the mixture of Se NPs and Cu2+ (curves g′ and g) was close to that after the addition of Se NPs (curves e′ and e). The results suggest that either Vc or Cu2+ had little influence on the Se NPs-induced fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs in the presence of Se NPs, Vc and Cu2+ were significantly recovered/turned on (curves h′ and h). Bai et al. has reported the reaction between Cu(II) and Se NPs in the presence of vitamin C [38]. Vc reduced Cu(II) to Cu(I) and the latter could be disproportionate to Cu(II) and Cu(0). (3) Cu II aq + reductive Vc → Cu I aq + oxidative Vc (4) 2 Cu I aq → Cu II aq + Cu 0 aq
The PET reaction of Cu(0) with Se NPs, which was similar to that between af-GQDs and Se0 (shown in Eq. (2)), was present. As a result, Cu2Se was finally generated on nanoSe0 according the following equation. (5) 2 Cu 0 aq + nano Se 0 sol → Cu 2 Se nanoSe 0 surface
In the present work, there was a competition between Cu(II) and af-GQDs. In the presence of Cu(II) and Vc, the amount of Se NPs that reacted with af-GQDs reduced. In other words, the PET between af-GQDs and Se NPs was inhibited once Cu2Se was produced. Therefore, the Se NPs-induced fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs was weakened.


### QUANTITATIVE DETECTION OF CU2+ ###

The fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs in the presence of the mixture composed of Se NPs and Vc (F 1) and that in the presence of the mixture comprising Se NPs, Vc and Cu2+ (F 2) were measured, respectively, and their difference, ΔF (ΔF = F 2 ⎻ F 1) was calculated. The ΔF values were recorded when pH and the mixing time of the Cu2+-Se NPs-Vc system changed and they are shown in Fig. 5. The fluorescence intensity change increased with increasing pH from 4.0 to 7.5. At a pH value greater than 7.5, the ΔF value decreased (panel A). Under the acidic condition (pH 4.0–7.0), protons could compete with Cu2+ for reacting with negatively charged nanoSe0, which caused the deficiency of Cu2Se on Se NPs surface. As a result, the Se NPs-induced fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs still appeared and the ΔF value was relatively small. Under the basic condition (pH > 7.5), it was easy for Vc to be oxidized by oxygen in air. In addition, Cu2+ might suffer from hydrolysis. These results were all unfavorable to the generation of Cu(I) and Cu2Se. Hence, pH 7.5 was employed in all the subsequent experiments, since the maximum ΔF value was achieved at this pH. The change in fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced as the mixing time increased from 0.5 h to 2.5 h (panel B), and it was almost invariable when the latter was further increased. Apparently the reactions described by Eqs. (3)–(5) required some time in which the adequate amount of Cu2Se would be produced on nanoSe0 surface. Thus, the optimal mixing time for Se NPs, Vc and Cu2+ was chosen at 2.5 h.
Af-GQDs were mixed with Se NPs, Vc and Cu2+ with different concentrations in pH 7.5 PBS. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture increased with increasing Cu2+ content, as depicted in Fig. 6. As shown in the insert, a good linear relationship existed between the ΔF value, which is defined in Fig. 5, and the logarithm of the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 1 nM-10 μM with the linear regression equation of ΔF = 85.5 + 114 log c Cu 2 + (R = 0.991). The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.4 nM at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The result suggests that the developed fluorescence method using af-GQDs and Se NPs could be used for sensitive detection of Cu2+. The fluorescent probes, linear ranges and detection limits of several fluorescent Cu2+ sensors [39–44] were listed in Table 1. The developed sensor in this work was found to be competitive in comparison with others.
The reproducibility of this assay was estimated at the Cu2+ concentrations of 50 nm and 1 μM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) corresponding to the five independent fluorescence measurement results (the ΔF values) were calculated to be 8.6% and 7.6%, respectively, showing a good reproducibility. In this work, af-GQDs were stored at 4 °C after the preparation. The af-GQDs suspension was ultrasonically re-dispersed before its use. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of these probes remained nearly 98% of the initial value after one month. This result exhibited a satisfying long-term stability of the prepared af-GQDs. The different ΔF values were obtained by measuring the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs in the presence of the mixture composed of Se NPs and Vc as well as that in the presence of the mixture comprising Se NPs, Vc and different metal ions, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 7. The ΔF value induced by Cu2+ was found to be much higher than that derived from other metal ions including Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, K+ and Na+. It means that these potential interfering substances had a little influence on the PET between af-GQDs and Se NPs. Therefore, the proposed fluorescence method displayed a high selectivity for the determination of Cu2+ in the presence of these metal ions.
The Cu2+ contents in tap water and lake water were measured by using the developed sensor. Table 2 shows the detection results from the samples before and after the addition of copper ions with known concentration. The concentrations of Cu2+ in the two water samples were estimated to be 1.12 and 1.63 μM, respectively, which were in good agreement with the results obtained from the atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) measurements, 1.09 and 1.69 μM. The recovery rates of Cu2+ added to these samples were in the range of 98.7–103%, indicating a satisfying accuracy.


### INVESTIGATION ON UPTAKE OF CU2+ INTO TUMOR CELLS ###

It has been reported that Cu transport at cell surface and Cu delivery to intracellular compartments are implicated in various biological processes [45]. In the present work, the uptake of copper ions into tumor cells and drug-resistant tumor cells was investigated based on the proposed fluorescence method. Fig. 8A and B show the microscope images of HeLa cells and HeLa/DDP cells in growth phase, separately. A monolayer of polygon-shaped HeLa cells, which were linked to each other, was observed on the substrate (panel A). It is interesting to be observed from panel B that the cisplatin-resistant cell line exhibited bigger spreading area than HeLa cells although the cell shape hardly altered. In this research, copper ions were added into pH 7.4 PBS, the HeLa cells-PBS suspension and the Hela/DDP cells-PBS suspension, respectively. The Cu2+-cells-PBS mixture suffered from centrifugal separation after different mixing time. The PBS containing copper ions (solution I), the PBS supernatant derived from the Cu2+-HeLa cells-PBS mixture (solutions II) and the PBS supernatant separated from the Cu2+-Hela/DDP cells-PBS mixture (solutions III) were used to inhibit the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs, respectively. The fluorescence-intensity change induced by solution I at the initial time (0 h) is marked as ΔF 0. The fluorescence-intensity difference caused by solutions I, II and III at the different time (0.5, 1, 4 and 7 h) is marked as ΔF 1. The ratios of ΔF 1 to ΔF 0 were defined as ΔF re and they are shown in Fig. 8C–E. Panel C shows that the ΔF re values induced by solution I at the different time remained basically unchanged. It indicates that copper ions at low concentration were stable in pH 7.4 PBS. One can observe from panels D and E that the ΔF re values caused by solutions II and III gradually declined with the increasing time. The results demonstrate the slow uptake of Cu2+ into the two kinds of tumor cells. The longer the mixing time of Cu2+ with tumor cells was, the less metal ions were remained in the PBS supernatant. As a result, the influence of copper ions on the Se NPs-induced fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs was weakened in sequence. In addition, the ΔF re value induced by solution II was found to be less than that caused by solution III at the same time, which means that fewer copper ions were present in solution II. In other words, the uptake amount of Cu2+ into HeLa cells was more than that into HeLa/DDP cells. It has been reported that Cu(II) is reduced to Cu(I) in the presence of extracellular Fe(III)/Cu(II) reductases, Frelp and Fre2p, which are encoded by the FRE1 and FRE2 genes, respectively, prior to copper uptake [46]. High affinity Cu(I) uptake is mediated by Cu transporters encoded by the CTR1 and CTR3 genes [47]. Human copper transporter 1 (hCTR1) is located in a cytoplasmic vesicular compartment and it is concentrated around the nucleus in the cervical carcinoma cell line HeLa [48]. It has been found that hCTR1 can modulate the influx of Pt(II)-based anticancer drugs such as cisplatin and carboplatin [49]. Our experimental results derived from Fig. 8 suggest that hCTR1 was down-regulated in the cisplatin-resistant tumor cells, HeLa/DDP cells. This down regulation, which might be derived from the protein internalization from plasma membrane [50], had a negative effect on the influx of cisplatin. It should be one of important reasons for the drug-resistance of HeLa/DDP cells. At the same time, the uptake of copper ions into HeLa/DDP cells was inevitably weakened due to the decreased hCTR1 expression level.


### CONCLUSIONS ###

For the determination of copper ions, af-GQDs were prepared via CA pyrolysis, using a three-step method, and Se NPs were synthetized via the reduction of selenious acid, using sodium alginate as a soft template. Af-GQDs exhibited strong fluorescence intensity and it could be effectively quenched by the latter due to photo-induced electron transfer. The generation of Cu2Se on nanoSe0 in the presence of copper ions and Vc had an inhibition effect on the PET between af-GQDs and Se NPs. The fluorescence intensity change of af-GQDs in the presence of Se NPs and Vc depended upon the Cu2+ concentration. Therefore, these prepared nanomaterials show a great promise in the development of a sensitive fluorescence sensor for the determination of copper ions. The uptake amount of Cu2+ into HeLa/DDP cells was found to be less than that into HeLa cells, which might be derived from the down-regulated hCTR1 content in these cisplatin-resistant tumor cells.

### TITLE ###

Ratio fluorometric determination of ATP base on the reversion of fluorescence of calcein quenched by Eu(III) ion using carbon dots as reference


### AUTHOR GROUP ###

Cengceng Zhang
Hui Zhang
Yuanyuan Yu
Shu Wu
Fang Chen


### ABSTRACT ###

A kind of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) with excellent stable luminescence performance was prepared by pyrolysis using ethanolamine as precursor. By simply mixing solution of NCDs and calcein-Eu3+, a ratio fluorometric probe with carbon dots as "internal reference" and calcein-Eu3+ as recognition group was constructed for ATP detection. The fluorescence of the calcein can be selectively quenched by Eu3+, and can be restored when ATP was added because Eu3+ ions exhibit higher affinity to the oxygen-donor atoms originated from phosphates than that from carboxylate groups. Meanwhile, fluorescence of NCDs was not affected by Eu3+, calcein or ATP. By adding NCDs as "internal reference" in the above system, a new ratiometric strategy for detecting ATP was conducted. The dynamic linear range for ATP detection was 5.0 × 10−8 mol L−1~ 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, and the detection limit was 2.0 × 10−8 mol L−1.The method was successfully applied to detecting ATP in adenosine disodium triphosphate injection. Compared with calcein- Eu3+ probe without NCDs as reference, the ratio fluorometric probe effectively reduced interference and improved the accuracy and sensitivity.


### ABSTRACT ###



### GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT ###

fx1


### KEYWORDS ###

NCDs
Calcein
Eu3+
ATP
Ratio fluorometric probe


### INTRODUCTION ###

As a common energy rich phosphate compound in cells, adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) provide energy directly for most of cellular events such as active transport, enzymatic processes and muscle contraction [1]. In the meanwhile, excessive wastage of ATP can be closely tied with some diseases, including cancer, ischemia and hypoglycemia [2,3]. Hence, various methods have been proposed for the determination of ATP by colorimetry [4,5], chemiluminescence [6,7], electrochemical assay [8], fluorescence [9–11]. By reason of high sensitivity and specificity, fluorescence has been being applied widely. For example, Yu et al. [10] found that the energy transfer of the ternary complex prulifloxacin-ATP-Tb3+ can more easily occurs due to effect of packing and cooperation. The fluorescence of prulifloxacin-Tb3+ increased greatly by adding ATP owing to the formation of ternary complex via electrostatic interaction and oxygen atoms in phosphate ions. Fang et al. [11] mixed carboxyfluorescein-labeled ATP aptamers and graphene oxide, causing the fluorescence quenching of carboxyfluorescein. The fluorescence of carboxyfluorescein was recovered by the addition of ATP because the stronger affinity between ATP and ATP aptamers caused desorption of the aptamers and graphene oxide.
Ratio fluorometric probes can emit two or more distinguishable wavelength at a single excitation wavelength. Using the ratio of fluorescence intensity at different wavelengths as analysis signal can effectively eliminate background interference and enhance precision and stability [12]. Several studies had been described applications of ratiometric fluorescent methods for ATP detection. Tang et al. [13] designed a rhodamine-based ratio fluorometric probe for the determination of ATP. Upon the addition of ATP, the ring of rhodamine spirolactam opened, leading to naphthalimide green emission decreased and rhodamine red emission appeared. An et al. [14] exploited a FRET probe for the detection of ATP that is based on the binding-induced modulation of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) coupled with the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) sensing mechanism. After adding ATP, the formation of polythiophene/ATP complex made the polythiophene skeleton from a random coil to the planar conformation and aggregation.
Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of novel carbon nanomaterials with excellent physical and chemical properties, such as photo-stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and high aqueous stability [15,16]. CDs have been extensively used as bio-imaging marker, sensors and adsorbent [17–21]. Recently there are some reports about CDs-based ratio fluorometric probe [22]. Among that, CDs can act as reference signal [23,24], energy donor [25,26], response signal [27] and dual-emission matrix [28–31]. Amjadi et al. [23] conducted a ratio fluorometric sensor for sensitive and selective determination of diniconazole, in which CDs entrapped in silica core serve as the reference signal and CdTe/CdS quantum dots exert analytical signal. After adding diniconazole, fluorescence of CdTe/CdS quantum dots is selectively quenched but CDs have scarcely changes. A ratio fluorometric probe, which included CDs as sensing signal and Rhodamine B as reference, for detection of Hg2+ and glutathione has been designed by Lu et al. [27] The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Hg2+ and recovered gradually by glutathione while fluorescence of Rhodamine B was not affected.
In this paper, the highly luminescent stable N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by pyrolysis method only using ethanolamine as precursor. By simply mixing NCDs, calcein and Eu3+ solutions, a ratio fluorometric probe with NCDs as reference and calcein-Eu3+ as analytical signal for ATP detection was proposed. As shown in Scheme 1, solution containing NCDs and calcein exhibited two emission peaks at 428 nm and 515 nm. After adding Eu3+, the fluorescence of calcein at 515 nm was quenched effectively while that of NCDs at 428 nm had no change. Sequentially, the fluorescence of calcein-Eu3+ was regained with the addition of ATP due to the stronger interaction between ATP and Eu3+, while the fluorescence of NCDs remained invariable. Hence, using ratio of fluorescence intensity at 515 nm and 428 nm as output signal, an “on-off-on” fluorescent method for the determination of ATP was established. The ratio fluorometric probe showed low interference, high accuracy and high sensitivity compared with calcein-europium probe without NCDs as reference.


### EXPERIMENTAL ###



### INSTRUMENTATIONS ###

The fluorescence spectra were attained on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). ZEN 3690 Zeta Potentiometer (Malvern Instruments Co., Ltd, Malvern, UK) and Tecnai G2 20 Transmission Electron Microscope (FEI Company, Eindhoven, The Netherlands) was applied for characterization of NCDs. TG16-WS high speed centrifuge (Changsha Xiangzhi Centrifuge Instrument Co., Ltd., Changsha, China) was used for separation of NCDs. Buffer solution was adjusted by pHS-3C Thunder Magnetic Acidity Meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).


### MATERIALS AND REAGENTS ###

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. Ethanolamine, Nitric acid, Calcein, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and H2SO4 (98%) were supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Adenosine triphosphate were purchased from Wuhan Huashun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China), Trishydroxymethylaminomethane was obtained from Shanghai Huajie Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Doubly deionized water was used throughout.


### ONE-STEP SYNTHESIS OF N-DOPED CARBON QUANTUM DOTS ###

N-doped CDs were prepared by pyrolyzing Ethanolamine described in the literature [32]. Briefly, 3.0 mL Ethanolamine and 4.5 mL H2O2 were added into a 200 mL beaker and mixed homogeneously. Then, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 30 min in an oven to brown viscous liquid. The product was added into 100 mL H2O. Finally, the N-doped CDs were collected via centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 30 min.


### ATP DETECTION IN A REAL SAMPLE ###

200 μL 1.35 mg mL−1 NCDs solution, 200 μL 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 Calcein solution, 1 mL Tris-HCl buffer(pH=7.3) and 200 μL 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 Eu3+ solution were transferred into a colorimetric tube. Then, 20 μL of standard solution or treated sample solution containing ATP was added into the tube and finally diluted to 10 mL with distilled water.
The fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and emission wavelengths of 428 nm (NCDs) and 515 nm (Calcein). The excitation and emission slits was set as 10 nm and 4.5 nm. The speed of scan was 400 nm min−1. Each sample was measured in parallel three times.


### RESULTS AND DISCUSSION ###



### CHARACTERIZATION OF NCDS ###

NCDs were synthesized by a one-step thermal decomposition method using Ethanolamine as the precursor at 180 °C for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 1A, the as-prepared NCDs exhibited a spherical or spheroidal morphology and were equally distributed, relatively. Infrared spectrum (IR) implied that the existence of functional groups on the surface of the NCDs, mainly -OH, -NH2 -CONH, which enabled the NCDs good water solubility (see in the Supplementary material, Fig. S1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis data (Fig. S2) consistent with results described by FT-IR spectrum, illustrated that N was efficiently doped in CDs, and -OH, -NH2, -CONH groups are on the surface of NCDs. The NCDs solution stored at 4 °C for several months remained stable without deposition.
The optical properties of NCDs were Characterized using fluorescence spectrum and UV–vis spectroscopy. As indicated in Fig. 1C, NCDs solution possessed a strong absorption peak and can emit bright blue fluorescence exposed to UV lamp (365 nm) but exhibit weak yellow in the sunlight. The fluorescent intensity of NCDs was first increased and then decreased when the excitation wavelength changed from 320 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 1D). That indicated different emission centers were existed because of several groups on the surface of CDs and the dominant emission centers will alternate as the excitation wavelength changed [33]. Meanwhile, the maximum fluorescence peak was at 360 nm of excitation wavelength and the emission peak is at 440 nm.


### LUMINESCENCE STABILITY OF NCDS ###

We explored effects of external environment on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs, such as pH value, mental ions, time of store and exposure time under UV light. As shown in Fig. S3A, the FL intensity of NCDs changed a lot under strong acid and alkali conditions. But at pH range from 4 to 9, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was basically constant. Except for the weak quenching of NCDs by Cu2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+, the presence of other metal ions did not affect the fluorescence of NCDs (Fig. S3B). As seen in Fig. S3C, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs stored for 12 months were only decreased a bit. Also, it possessed good photo-bleaching resistance under continuous illumination with UV light (λ = 365 nm), which was shown in Fig. S3D. The fluorescence intensity was decayed slightly with exposure time increased to 3 h, which remained more than 90% of initial intensity. These suggested that the as-prepared NCDs have excellent stability.


### SPECTRAL PROPERTIES OF NCDS AND CALCEIN ###

Fig. S4 shows the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of NCDs and calcein. As shown in Fig. S4A, the absorption peak of calcein remained almost constant after adding NCDs，and the absorption spectrum of mixture (curve c) were similar to curve d (sum of curve a and curve c). Fluorescence spectra (Fig. S4B) showed that the fluorescence of NCDs was stable when mixed with calcein. But the fluorescence of calcein decreased apparently for internal filtering. These also suggested that the NCDs have excellent stability, which confirmed that the fluorescence of NCDs are stable enough and can be used as “internal reference” in the following study.


### EFFECTS OF EU3+ AND ATP ON THE FLUORESCENCE OF CALCEIN AND NCDS ###

Firstly, the effects of Eu3+ and ATP on the fluorescence of calcein were studied. As depicted in Fig. 2A, calcein possessed a strong fluorescence peak at 525 nm(curve a). After Eu3+ added, the fluorescence of calcein quenched due to the interaction between calcein and Eu3+ (curve b). Continually, the fluorescence of calcein can be obviously recovered by ATP for its strong coordination ability towards Eu3+(curve c). According to the fluorescence recovery of calcein, a method for ATP can be established.
To verify the feasibility of NCDs as “internal standard” in the above system for ATP detection, the fluorescence of NCDs-Calcein-Eu3+ in the absence and presence of ATP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2B, mixture of NCDs-Calcein had two emission peak at 428 nm and 515 nm (curve a). After Eu3+ was added, the fluorescence of calcein at 428 nm was markedly quenched whereas the fluorescence of NCDs at 515 nm was almost constant (curve b). With ATP added, the fluorescence of calcein recovered whereas the NCDs’ still remained the same (curve c). Therefore, by using fluorescence ratio of 515 nm to 428 nm (F515/F428) as signal, a fluoremetric method can be constructed for the determination of ATP.


### OPTIMIZATION OF CONDITIONS FOR ATP DETERMINATION ###



### EFFECT OF PH ###

The Effect of pH on fluorescence of NCDs-Calcein-Eu3+ in the absence and presence of ATP was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. S5, fluorescence ratio of 515 nm to 428 nm(F515/F428) of NCDs-calcein-Eu3+ increased with the increasing of pH (curve a). These might be due to the hydrolysis of Eu3+ in alkaline environment and prohibiting of fluorescence quenching [34]. The fluorescence of NCDs-Calcein only can be effectively quenched by Eu3+ within pH 7.1–7.6. The Effect of pH on fluorescence of NCDs-Calcein-Eu3+ in presence of ATP (Fig. S5 curve b) showed that the fluorescence recovered apparently while pH within 7.1–7.6, and decreased with the increasing of pH. These might because the interaction between Eu3+ and ATP also was restricted in alkaline condition. Therefore, buffer solution of pH 7.3 was chosen for the detection of ATP.
Influence of Eu3+ concentration
The quenching fluorescence of calcein by different Eu3+ concentration was also studied ( Fig. 3). The FL intensity of calcein at 515 nm decreased with the increasing of Eu3+ concentration and not decreased while the concentration of Eu3+ higher than 1.2 × 10−6 mol L−1, suggesting that a stable complex had formed between calcein and Eu3+. So 1.2 × 10−6 mol L−1 was chosen as the best Eu3+ concentration for the following study.


### INFLUENCE OF REACTION TIME ###

As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence of calcein can reached a maximum value in 10 min and kept almost constant more than 40 min. So, 15 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for the system.


### ANALYTICAL APPLICATION ###



### PERFORMANCE OF RATIO FLUOROMETRIC PROBES AND COMPARISON ###

Under the optimized condition, the analytical performance of the probe with and without NCDs was carefully studied. As depicted in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of calcein at 515 nm increased gradually with ATP added whereas the NCDs at 428 nm was nearly constant (Fig. 4A). A good linear relationship between F515 / F428 and the concentration of ATP was obtained in the range of 5.0 × 10−8 mol L−1 to 2.0 × 10 −6 mol L−1. The equation was F515/F428 = 0.609 + 0.252CATP (C: ×10−6 mol L−1，R2 = 0.9988，n = 10). The detection limit was calculated to be 2.0 × 10−8 mol L−1 (Fig. 4B) and the relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) was 0.33% for determination of 5.0 × 10−8 mol L−1 ATP. Simultaneously, the probe without NCDs as reference was explored (Fig. 4C and D). The result suggested that the linear range was only 1.0 × 10−7 to 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, and the correlation coefficient of calibration curve was 0.96, which was much lower than ratio fluorometric methods using NCDs as reference. These confirmed that the proposed ratio fluorometric method can eliminate environmental influence to make it wider linear range, higher sensitive and higher precision.


### ATP DETERMINATION FOR REAL SAMPLE ###

To verify the feasibility, the ratio fluorometric probe (NCDs-calcein- Eu3+) was used for detection of ATP in adenosine disodium triphosphate injection. As shown in Table 1, the concentration of ATP in the diluted sample was 3.39 × 10−7 mol L−1 (RSD = 6.9%, n = 3), and the calculated concentration of original sample was 10.25 mg mL−1, which was the same with the labeling value (10 mg mL−1). The recoveries for detection were between 96% and 109% and the relative standard deviations were below 3%, which indicated the applicability of ATP determination in real sample.


### CONCLUSIONS ###

In summary, the NCDs with stable emission properties were prepared successfully by one-step pyrolysis using ethanolamine used as carbon source and nitrogen source. A ratio fluorometric probe for ATP detection was established by mixing NCDs and calcein-Eu3+. The fluorescence of calcein quenched by Eu3+ can recovered while ATP added, whereas fluorescence of NCDs not changed. Compared with calcein-Eu3+ probe without NCDs as reference, the ratio fluorometric probe can reduce interference availably and improve accuracy and sensitivity. This kind of NCDs with stable emission properties might be widely applied to various ratio fluorometric methods by using fluorescence of NCDs as “internal reference”.


### SUPPORTING INFORMATION ###

Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.01.062
